lines.x: The streets of Columbia, as a whole, are generally more spacious and vibrant than those of Siracusa.
But when walking through these streets, one sometimes feels a sense of emptiness, as if one does not belong.
Hey you, carwash guy. Heard you're from Siracusa, so I got a question for you.
I'm here to wash cars, not answer questions.
Don't be like that, pal.
Relax, we're not here to cause trouble for broke-ass bumpkins like you.
Heard a group of your countrymen came into town today, and we just wanna know where they are.
What business do you have with them?
Business? Uhh... catching up on old times?
Old times, huh.
Hey, my nonno always used to tell me, 'never forget that you're Siracusan.'
You don't look 'Siracusan' to me.
Me? Siracusan? Hah!
Columbia is the future, pal! It's where the gold certificates are!
The young associates of the Columbian families always heard from their forefathers that they came from Siracusa.
But they've never taken it seriously.
Your roots will always be in Siracusa.
It's the crusty old fossils who say stuff like that.
Columbia is a land of pioneers! Roots? We don't need roots.
That's right, hick. You'd better tell us where your paesani are, if you don't want any 'accidents' happening to your little shop here.
They're in that bar across the street.
You got a good head on your shoulders, pal. Don't see a whole lotta wolf pups out there as bright as you.
To be a pioneer is to invite chaos, and the Columbian families gradually lost the order they should've had amidst this chaos.
But for Siracusa, order stands taller than the law itself.
The carwash worker zones out their noise and commotion, turning and slowly opening a cabinet to produce a tool that he is all too familiar with.
It is heavy and sharp, having been meticulously polished by the carwash worker. The glint reflecting off its blade reminds one of the moonlight in Siracusa.
Then, he slowly walks toward the complacent idiots who have their backs turned to him.
Like a carwash worker who only wants to scrub the floors before opening up shop.
Like a Siracusan.
Texas's eyes snap wide open.
As her vision comes into focus in the darkness, she sees a poster Exusiai put up against her will.
Her hands transmit to her the sensation of the down quilt that Croissant sold to her at a special price when the season changed.
Sora's sleep-assisting CD is still playing.
All of this lets her breathe a sigh of relief.
She understands full well that the dream had never really happened, that the Columbian family never had a chance to pester the Siracusan guest.
As for the carwash worker, she has only met him once.
A dream that has no meaning except to remind her of her Siracusan roots.
Since coming to Lungmen, she hasn't had this dream in a long while.
So why have it now?
She unconsciously wants to see the night outside her window.
The moonlight still shines bright and clean.
Myriad colors still dot Lungmen's nightscape.
But within it all is an existence utterly incompatible with everything around it.
Outside her window, on a rooftop beneath the moon, a wolf is staring directly at her.
If everything surrounding it can be called the creations of civilization, then this wolf is undoubtedly a symbol of the wild wastes.
He does not belong here, and never should have appeared here.
But he still appeared in all his majesty, his figure seeming to tower above everything else around him.

lines.st: Come. It is time for you to fulfill your end of the deal.

lines.x: In an instant, Texas understands why she had the dream.
Dreams are not necessarily connected to reality.
But dreams often are omens.
And without a doubt, the dream she had tonight portends—
That her past has come knocking on her door.
Noisy. Excessive. Corrupt.
How far you've fallen, to become indolent in a place like this.
But, at least you thought of killing me the moment you saw me.
That means my trip was still worthwhile.
I can give it a serious try, Zaaro.
It won't make a difference in the end. Same as seven years ago.
You should feel grateful, last wolf of Famiglia Texas. Our deal still stands, which means you still have a chance to pay off your debt.
The wastelands have always played fair.
Stop calling yourself the wastelands.
It has never cut deals with anyone, 'Signore dei Lupi.'
Heh. Maybe you've just never gotten close enough.
Stop talking in circles. What do you want me to do?
Follow me back to Siracusa. You are to assist my Fang.
...'Fang.'
Fulfill his request, and consider your debt paid in full.
At that time, whether you choose to return here or remain there is no longer any of my concern.
Fine.
If it ain't Zaaro, Lord of Wolves.
Think you can just show up in my city, take my people, leave like it ain't no thing?
Your brain overgrown with weeds now too? Ah, my bad. Forgot, you ain't even got a brain.
Emperor, my wretched kin.
How long has it been since we last met? Two hundred years? Three?
What is this revolting outfit of yours? What are you dressing up as?
What happened to your sheet music? Or your conductor's baton? Have you finally tired of pretentiously tickling the ivories atop the lofty spires of Leithanien?
Mind your business, Zaaro. As if I'd remember when the last time we met was. Wish there wasn't a last time, hope there won't be a next time, and that I forget about this time.
I don't understand. You can choose to step aside, and then forget all about it.
Did I stutter? Get your sorry ass back to Siracusa, and leave my people outta your shit.
I don't even get how your brothers and sisters still let you and your pups play your little mob game in Siracusa.
There are many paths to victory. This was my decision, and it has nothing to do with you.
Y'all had some kinda inside deal there, right?
Like y'all keep to your own turf, keep outta human business.
Take a good look at yourself. You hardly look any different from the humans, and you're telling me to stay out of human affairs?

What are these toys of yours to the glaciers that have not melted for tens of thousands of years? What are these ridiculous black glasses of yours compared to the lasting darkness of the polar nights?
And then, there's your so-called 'music.' What need is there for music when you already have the coldest wind?
Throw all of this away, Emperor.
You are forgetting what you truly are. What 'we' truly are.
You made up those rules. They ain't for me.
My life ain't your business, wolfie.
They came up with something new, I decided I liked it, and I made it mine. Simple as that.
We've always belonged to the wasteland.
You showed your ass callin' this land the 'wastes.' Ain't nothin' wasted here to me.
Think you're any different from the humans? Rollin' around the forests of Siracusa didn't get you all hungry for power. You never thought about where all that ambition came from?
Zaaro, you ain't made no progress in millennia, and on top of that, you also picked up the dumbest shit humanity's got on offer.
(Low snarl)
Stow your fangs, kid. That's rude.
There's no point fighting amongst ourselves. Nothing will come of it.
I'm just a messenger, here to claim what's rightfully mine.
She isn't even qualified to be my pawn yet.
My people are more than qualified to do anything. Including guttin' you.
Your people?
She doesn't belong to you, Emperor.
Perhaps, you can ask her opinion.
Seven years ago, when I wanted out of Columbia, Zaaro struck a deal with me.
I'd be allowed to go free, and in return, I would do him one favor, in the future.
Without this promise, I wouldn't have made it out of Columbia alive.
Ugh, I know all that. Point is, you gonna leave with him?
Sorry, boss, but I have to honor my word.
I don't give no shit about deals and words, Zaaro. She's my employee.
Texas, I could, magnanimous man that I am, give you some vacation time and let you sort through all your business. Got nothin' to do with me.
I mean, Penguin Logistics employees are free, even if they're in Siracusa.
And you, Zaaro, if you dare get up in her business, I'll show up at your stinkin' lair and rip the tail hairs outta you and every one of your siblings. Make myself a nice broom.
Hah.
Child of Texas, you may make preparations as you see fit, or say your goodbyes.
No need. Sorry, boss, but I'm going to need a favor. Tell the girls I'm going to be gone for a while, but I'll be back soon.
I'll just tell 'em you were cryin' about wanting to head back to Siracusa, and you took a long vacation. I tried to stop you, but you wouldn't take no for an answer.
Then... I'll have to buy them some extra souvenirs, and apologize to them when I get back.
If you have nothing to say, let's be off.
Fine.
And so, a woman and a wolf walk westward, soon disappearing into the night.
The streetlights flicker on and off.
Volsinii Alleyway
*pant*... *pant*... Did I make it?
Heh, good question.
When did you—?!
You're pretty lucky. Right when took the shot, you bent over to pick up the keys that had fallen under the seat.
Wh-Who are you people? What did I ever do to you?!
'Did I make it?' Hah! 'Did I make it?'
Can you make it away from air and water? Can a Siracusan ever get away from the rainy season?
Who could ever get away from them? Or well, from us?
What famiglia do you belong to? I've done everything right. No gambling, no debt. I'm just an ordinary clerk at il Ministero dei Lavori Pubblici!
Ohh, il Ministero dei Lavori Pubblici. That's a hot topic around Volsinii these days.
I... I know the son of Don Bellone!
That's enough talking.
(Now's my chance!)
Ugh, what a pain in the ass. A little trick won't save you.
The rain's not letting up, huh.
Want a bite? After all, this is Siracusa, the land of pizza!
I've ordered all the flavors the gourmet guide said were worth ordering!
At least one of us is in a good mood.
You didn't forget why we came here, didja?
Not like worrying about it's gonna do any good.
We've been asking around. Those guys in the black suits don't ever say anything!
All I heard is that some family by the name of Bellone recently welcomed a guest. I'm gonna head over and take a look around their place.
That scene, though, tsk tsk. The fowlbeasts are probably gonna make a detour once they pass by.
Ain't that all the more reason to worry?
Lemme tell you, our girl's from here. With her skills, she won't get into any big trouble.
Plus, we came here with the expectation that we might not see her face-to-face at all. Isn't that right, Sora?
Mhm.

Plays and operas are a very robust scene in Siracusa. Whether you're in a tavern or in a theater, there are always many performances to be found. Siracusans from all walks of life regard opera as a way to unwind.
They say even the mobsters are no exception.
So I thought, maybe if I could get into a troupe...
Gotta hand it to ya, Sora, quick thinkin' there nabbin' this chance through Monster Siren Records.
Coincidentally, my agent just asked if I was interested in going to Siracusa to develop my career, which I turned down because I didn't want to leave Lungmen.
But even so, ain't Siracusan opera way different from yer usual style?
It sure is. Both the choreography and the singing will pose major challenges. Plus, we're also talking about a completely different kind of stage.
But after seeing my performance, the director said he'd be willing to give me a shot.
Aha... If MSR didn't already have a contact ready to go, I would've had to keep waiting.
Are you sure I'm not gonna wake up to find you also disappeared without saying goodbye?
That's not going to happen!
I'm not even brave enough for that.
Speakin' of which, ain't it time for your meetup with the troupe's artistic director?
You always had these meetings in some kinda company building or theater before. Crazy he wanted to meet ya in a restaurant like this.
That's not completely unheard of. Some people prefer to chat in a more natural, everyday setting to get a better feel for the actor.
An everyday setting, huh.
Poppin' up in Siracusa like this don't feel natural to me in the least. But, all I'n do is hope it goes well.
When we get back to Lungmen, Texas is payin' for three months of boba tea... No, make that six!
Only six? We've got a chance to make her pay through the nose!
Wow, the director actually showed.
H-Help me!
Huh? What's going on?
Look out!
Spectacular! I got some great shots from the rehearsal just now!
How was it, Signor Bernardo?
Not bad.
Really?!
But, not good either.
You're too eager to perform, Lena. It's as if you want ten critics to see twelve different emotions in one of your performances.
But, that's too excessive for my needs.
Just give me one thing, as long as it's yours. Your own grief, your own passion, your own desolation.
It's not easy being an actor. You are not a prop on the stage, Lena, and you should not settle for being a prop either.
S-Si, signor!
Director, the guests from Lungmen have arrived.
Very good.
Are you sure you need to meet with her personally though? She's just a small-time songstress from Lungmen. I can handle this myself.
Loreno, I know that you, as Lena's uncle, have always sought to impede her potential competitors.
But you could try to hide it a little better.
Wait... I'm not trying to hide anything from you at all. It's just that we don't have a particularly deep relationship with Monster Siren Records.
Rather, there isn't a single theater troupe in Siracusa who thinks highly of this agency trying so desperately to wriggle into our market.
But you went beyond expectations in agreeing to the deal to bring one of their singers over to study at our theater.
Both logically and emotionally, this doesn't benefit us at all. And now you're even going to meet her, personally?
It's as you said, Loreno. She is just a small-time songstress from Lungmen, with nothing inherently deserving of my attention.
But, as you will soon see, the 'from Lungmen' part holds a special significance.
Are you talking about...?
(*whispers*)
Thank you. Tell him, don't go overboard.
It's our life, isn't it?
These guys are actually using explosive bolts?!
Are you alright, sir?
I-I'm fine, for now.
But you people... you're foreigners, right?
You shouldn't have helped me. Getting mixed up with those people is a good way to get killed.
Am I the only one who thinks it's mighty strange that there's a murder in broad daylight and nobody seems to care?!
Grr, these guys are tougher to deal with than the gangsters back in Lungmen.
You're only making trouble for yourself, straniera.
What a coincidence. The thing I'm second best at is making trouble for myself.
By the way, the thing I'm best at... is getting rid of those troubles~
You're in Siracusa now.
So what?
So that means you have no idea what you're getting yourself into.
Watch out, Exusiai!
Attacking civilians! Which famiglia are you with?
Have you forgotten our ironclad laws?
Ugh... Talk about bad timing...
No wonder this little runt kept running toward Lavinia's commute.
Let's get outta here.
Get back here!
Judge Lavinia!
I remember you. You're from il Ministero dei Lavori Pubblici... What's going on?
I-I have no idea! I was just heading to work!
From the way those guys were dressed up, they should've known the rules. Then, why...
I'm just a law-abiding citizen. I've never even gotten mixed up with any of the famiglie! Why, why are they still...
Your Honor, don't you think there's been something weird in the air recently?
Are the three of you alright?
I'm fine, but those guys ended up getting away...
Heh, I never expected Siracusa's customs to be so cutthroat.
Foreigners in Siracusa should try to be a bit more careful.
And who might you be?
I am Lavinia, a judge for this city. What about you?

You can call me Exusiai. And she's Sora.
And that's Croissant over there.
First of all, I would like to thank you. Your brave actions saved the life of this innocent civilian.
Need my help chasing those guys down?
Don't bother. They know this city like the backs of their hands, and you won't ever catch them. I'll sort out the rest.
Where are you from?
We came from Lungmen.
Might as well give you our business card. If'n ya ever need somethin' delivered, you give us a holler~
Lungmen, Penguin Logistics... You're with a logistics company?
That's right! No matter what it is, a tiny coin or a big ol' burdenbeast, we can take it wherever you need it to go!
Day or night, rain or shine, the customer's satisfaction is our mission!
Oh, assuming you have the money to pay, that is!
...Pfft.
Huh? Is our pitch that funny?
No, I'm sorry. I've just been a bit on edge lately.
Did you come to Volsinii to expand your business, or just for pleasure?
Either way, you chose an unfortunate time to come.
Well, actually, we came lookin' fer a friend of ours...
Lavinia, we're about to enjoy a grand celebration. What better time could there be to visit?
A new city is about to be born in Volsinii—the greatest event in the history of Siracusa.
Should they be lucky enough, our new friends may get to witness its birth.
...Bernardo.
Don't worry. These are my guests.
Yours? Or your troupe's?
Which would you prefer?
I hope... it's neither.
That's too bad then, because the answer is both.
It's not too late to go back to Lungmen right now, Exusiai.
Whaddya mean?
You always get the wrong idea about me, Lavinia.
I wasn't even the one who invited them here. Signorina Sora chose to come to Volsinii herself. Isn't that right?
Yessir. In fact, I'm grateful to Compagnia dell'alba for being willing to take me.
Alright then. Since you're Signor Bernardo's... guests, you won't have to worry much about your safety, at the very least.
Just, please contact me if you need something, alright? My number's right here.
Can do~
Now then, I need to ask this gentleman about what happened to him. The rest of you... enjoy your chat.
Bernardo... Bernardo... Oh!!
Wait, are you the Bernardo, artistic director of Compagnia dell'alba?
That I am.
Oh wow... You came all the way out here just for us...
I've always chosen actors myself. After all, you know what they say about wanting a job done well.
And, who might these two be?
They're my good friends who came to Siracusa with me.
We're her bodyguards, too. The name's Croissant.
And you can just call me Exusiai.
Very interesting names.
Shouldn't we be... moseyin' along?
There's no need. The famiglia associates don't cause trouble without reason. I'm not sure what kind of mess that clerk got himself into, but Siracusa is not without its own order.
This ain't your typical 'order' now, is it?
You think so? I got the impression our Sankta friend here would be quite familiar with it.
Nah, even in Laterano, something like that would've been considered straight-up assault!
Hah, the Signora of Siracusa always says she brought 'guns and order' from Laterano, but the reality seems to be a bit different.
The customs here might be a bit too extreme for outsiders. But, if you plan on spending any amount of time here, you'll want to get used to them as quickly as possible.
Setting aside the little intermezzo we just enjoyed, Signorina Sora, is now a good time to discuss your new job?
Oh, of course!
I'm assuming you haven't read the script yet, correct? This is our troupe's latest masterpiece, and your role is found within.
I-It would be an honor...
We haven't finalized the title of this production yet, but the name our creative team has chosen for this tragedy in three acts is—
'La Morte di Texas.'
Wh-What did you just say?
Oh? Seems my play has already captured your attention. Let's take our time and talk.
Oh right, I almost forgot in all the commotion just now.
Benvenuti in Siracusa, mie amiche.
Grr... It just had to be Lavinia again. Rotten luck.
And there was that Sankta too. Why the hell would a bunch of foreigners just show up in Siracusa, at a time like this? Plus, she had some real skills too...
Think it might be true that Signora Sicilia's guy might already be in Volsinii?
Ugh, who cares. Let's head back and then—
Who's there?
Relax, paesani. I ain't your enemy.
Who do you belong to?
Hmm... If I had to say, I s'pose I'd call myself a Sicilian.
A Sicilian? Stop joking around. They got rubbed out ages ago.
Is that so?
I don't have the energy to waste my breath on you, so get outta my way, unless you got a death wish.
I'm just here to remind you there are people from the court waitin' in ambush over there. Steer clear, if you know what's good for you.
What?!
Who the hell are you? I never heard nothin' about nobody coming to talk to us.
Course you didn't. Because I didn't come here to talk to you.
That brings me to the second thing I'd like to remind you of—
The Sicilians may have been erased, but they didn't die out.
What—
Urk... Guh...
By the way, we came here to silence you.
You talk too damn much. Hope Lappland's not rubbing off on you.
That, we can agree on.
Hey, it's me. We're done here.
Stay in place and wait?
Grr, you're the boss. Ain't like I got a choice.
Looks like we chose the wrong day to come back.
Why do you say that?
Rain.
I hate the rain more than anything.
It's the rainy season. I already told you before we came back.
Now that you don't have your little brother trimming your fur for you, I think you oughta go across the street right now and get it all shaved off.
Watch it, or that mop's gonna come right off your scalp.
I actually was considering it myself, but my barber talked me out of it. Said my head's lopsided, I'd look ugly.
But, I gotta hand it to you for findin' a way to make money so quick.
Hmph. Looks like you spent too much time in Lungmen, Capone.
That Lin fella, sure he's got a few tricks up his sleeve and all, but doesn't Lungmen's governor have him pinned down pretty good?
But this is Siracusa. And here, the famiglie run the show.
It's easy to find an odd job or two.
With so many famiglie running around, everyone always needs some capable hands.
Alright, maybe I was in Lungmen too long, but what really surprises me is how they're still running the same playbook they used to when I left Siracusa.
The famiglie control everything in this city from the shadows, but they never walk out onto the stage.
Why do you say that?
A new city is about to be born. On the surface, they're in the middle of voting for its administrator.
But the truth is, the famiglie backing these people are constantly jockeying for power.
What the hell are you even talking about? If we didn't lose our own city, we'd be doin' the same exact thing.
I've been away from Siracusa for a long time, Gambino. Was it eight years? Ten?
That's a drop in the bucket. What's gonna change in that amount of time?
It's long enough to change the way I see a lotta things, but clearly not long enough to fix your shortsightedness.
You know, it ain't like we came to no peace agreement, you and me. Our work just isn't done yet.
If you're so eager to die, I'm glad to send you on your way.
How can you be sure that you won't be the one dying?
Hah...
Hmph...
What?!
How'd you know...?
Bartender, get your ass out here!
I didn't think the two of you were on such good terms.
Haha, looks like your patron ain't as reliable as you thought, Gambino.
Let's be clear here. As long as the two of you complete my commission, I naturally will not mistreat you.
However, the commission is not yet complete.
You think you can double-cross me?!
Double-cross? If this is your idea of a double-cross, then I recommend you open your eyes a bit wider, Sicilian.
So, where does that put us now?
I need to see what you two are capable of—right here, right now. It's that simple.
As long as you walk out of this alley alive, you pass.
I thought you were gonna say you'd take both of us at once.
Haha, my brothers would rake me over the coals for that one. 'You weren't going to let anyone pass in the first place,' or something like that.
You sure talk a big game.
I'm looking forward to meeting both of you again.
Pretentious bastard. Thinks he's human just because he got used to the city.
Hah... It's been a while since I've heard that.
Looks like we don't have a choice.
I'll make sure he figures out who doesn't have a choice.
Heh. If I knew I'd be fighting for my life back in Siracusa, I would've just stayed home.
Was that your call to make?
Alright, fine. If we didn't show up, we probably would've been turned into mille-feuille within a day.
But we followed her here, and she went and disappeared without a word.
As if she was saying, 'I brought you back home. Now you're free to do whatever you want, go fend for yourselves.'
The moment you start thinking that is when she pops up right in front of you. You've jinxed it.
You look like you got used to life here as well.
She will come for us.
Oh?
Lemme ask you this. In Siracusa, who's in bigger danger—a wolf who leaves the pack and goes rogue, or two strays?
Well that's new. Never thought you'd fess up to being a stray mutt.
I ain't dumb enough to act like I ain't lost everything.
Wish you'd admitted it sooner.
Still, it's pretty obvious that she's tryin' to play with fire right under Signora Sicilia's nose.
Right. We ain't even shit on the side of the road to that woman. And I won't stand for it. If I get the chance, I swear I'm gonna kill her.
But then there's her. She doesn't even give a damn about Signora Sicilia.
The same Signora Sicilia who sends us a letter and gets us shakin' in our boots.
And that wasn't enough to get her back to Siracusa. But now, she's back, all over another lone wolf.
Oh, what was it that she asked again? Right, how we ended up so scared of Signora Sicilia?
You scared of her, Capone?
Honestly, yeah. Nobody can really go against Signora Sicilia.
Every Siracusan's afraid of her.
So, you wanna see how she dies?
I wanna see how she lives.
Hate to admit it, but I'm with you.
That's why we need to stay alive.
Another civil servant got attacked? And a Bellone associate to boot?
Yeah, someone's clearly trying to pick a fight.
Hah, serves 'em right. If you ask me, it's probably the Rossatis behind it. Those scumbags and their bag of petty tricks. Disgusting.
But, a lot of the Dodici Famiglie have already said they support the Bellones taking over the new city.
So what's the point of pulling a stunt like this now?
Hmph, hard to say. The Don's son, Leontuzzo, is now a secretary at il Ministero dei Lavori Pubblici.
If someone has the skills to take him out along with il Ministro himself, wouldn't that blow things wide open again?
Considering that runt's dumb enough to set his own famiglia aside to be a yes-man for the government.
You say that, but are you forgetting how many associates ate shit over the last two years because they underestimated him? Oh, I almost forgot, you were one of them.
Bah. I'll admit, the runt's got some talent, but that's only because the don's been sitting on his hands the whole time! Once he decides to call the shots...
That's true. The don hasn't said anything yet, and the Bellones can't name themselves the winners so soon.
Enough.
The entire room suddenly falls silent, the clamor and chatter from before seeming like nothing more than an illusion.
I don't care what you believe deep down, but during this tense time, you don't make light of the situation, and you don't do anything rash.
I know what you're all thinking. I get it.
Of the Dodici Famiglie, only our Famiglia Saluzzo hasn't yet placed our wager.
It is still too soon for that.
Yes, Don Alberto.
Since time immemorial, Famiglia Saluzzo has always been like this.
One is allowed to think, but not to advise. Because Alberto Saluzzo calls all the shots.
Alberto never allows anyone to question his decisions.
Nobody in the famiglia finds this unusual. They say that the Saluzzos' strength comes from the fact that Alberto is always correct.
Everyone's here? My, now that I look closely, I see a lot of old faces.
You... You're...
But, that is not to say that there are no rebels. Only, most of them have been erased from the famiglia.
And were never heard from again.
With the exception of a white wolf named Lappland.
She pulls a chair over and props her feet up on the table, as if she owns the place.
Who let her in?
Don Alberto, sh-she killed everyone outside and forced her way in...
This is a famiglia meeting. Get her out of here.
Signorina, I mean, Lappland... The Don has asked you to leave.
I turned down Signora Sicilia's lunch invitation and rushed back like a good little fowlbeast, eager to return to its nest.
This hideout we have in Volsinii... it's kinda drab, compared to the old Saluzzo stronghold.
Tony, do you really have to treat me so cold?
You're no longer an associate of this famiglia.
What, you think I came back to beg the old man to let me back into this damned—I mean, this most distinguished famiglia?
Please watch your words, signorina.
Right, right. Even though I was so disgracefully kicked out, this famiglia has always had a special place in my heart.
So, at this critically important time, I have chosen to come back home, at risk to my own life, ignoring my father's disdainful glare, to bring back a vital piece of intelligence.
Does that not move you to tears?
Lappland... Even if you are the Don's own daughter, you've overstepped your bounds.
This is no place for you to run roughshod...
You... Nngh... Urgh...
Never seen that guy before. Was he new?
Y-Yes! S-Signorina Lappland.
You're so cruel, Father, to doubt the sincerity of my actions.
You got my desk dirty, Lappland.
Desks can be replaced, right?
Let's hear it then. Your so-called 'vital intelligence.'
Texas.
The same Texas you let go?
That's right. Bellone brought her back.
Lappland squints at her father.
This homecoming has not inspired a single shred of emotion within her.
She carries with her only a faint glimmer of amusement as to what might happen. Or rather, what she is about to make happen.
Because, she has been in such environments far too many times in her life.
She has returned.
The thoughts that inadvertently bubble up in her mind seem ever so laughable.
Celli——
Just call me Texas.
Alright, Texas. How does it feel to be back in Siracusa?
Not too great.
I can imagine. If I were you, I wouldn't feel too great about suddenly being forced to go somewhere, even if it was my homeland.

Though, if this isn't too much to ask, I'd like you to imagine this as well: your father suddenly forced a 'bodyguard' onto you, and she's a whole lot stronger than you. That's not a great feeling either.
Understandable.
That's what I wanted to hear.
Unfortunately, I don't have the power to let you leave.
All I can say is, I hope we can both do what we need to do.
Go ahead and get changed. We had this outfit custom-tailored in old Famiglia Texas style. I hope you can be my bodyguard tonight and accompany me to the banquet.
Sure.
Beside Texas lies the well-pressed suit that Famiglia Bellone has prepared for her.
She looks toward the tightly-shut door.
She knows what she is supposed to do.
To fulfill Zaaro's contract, she needs to exit this room, kill a few irrelevant people, create some chaos, and then finally, kill a few important people.
Irrelevant, or important. This is the attitude Siracusans have toward life.
She, too, used to think like that.
Even now that she has learned to see the world through a different lens, as soon as she stepped back into this city, she quickly realized that these practices were still ever so familiar to her.
She has returned.
The thoughts that inadvertently bubble up in her mind fill her with disgust.
Welcome to Teatro di Milano, signorine mie.
Wow, this place is breathtaking!

Siracusans love to kill time here to the accompaniment of music and performance. After all, when reality is monotonous and difficult to change, people naturally need a place to indulge their fantasies.
Or, to equate their bloodstained business dealings with the legendary exploits of the heroes on stage to obtain a sense of moral satisfaction.
Wow... you really don't pull any punches, huh?
Of course not. In this line of work, we must be clear-eyed about our own profession, no?
Your friends over there seem to have been on their guard from the very beginning.
I don't mean no disrespect, Mr. Director, but the way I heard it, you was callin' that play yer rehearsin'...
'La Morte di Texas.'
'The Death of Texas'? That might be the crappiest 'joke' I've ever heard.
Hm? You seem to be well-acquainted with that name. That's fairly unusual among foreigners.
Siracusans rarely bring it up in the first place, and the mafiosi here are even more secretive about it. But the story of Famiglia Texas... is one that every mafioso must always keep in their hearts.
It is a cautionary tale.
A warning...
Let's talk about the script itself first. There are countless works based on the deeds of Famiglia Texas, but not a single one dares to actually print the name.
Playwrights are more inclined to reimagine or adapt, with the help of allusion or parody. After all, it is against their interests to invite trouble.
However, times change, and today's audience finds true history far more salient than fairy tales with falsified names. Wouldn't you agree?
As such, a remarkable individual has brought this script before us.
It's a play in three acts.
The first act harkens back to the era when Columbia was first established, with many a Siracusan famiglia yearning for this undeveloped land.
Consequently, the famiglie each dispatched their own teams, one after another, joining the ranks of Columbia's first pioneers.
'In an era when chaos and opportunity came hand-in-hand, some were swallowed by the tides of the times, while others found in it their promised land.'
The cream of the crop among them was Salvadore Texas, a man who would later establish his very own era.
(I know he means the surname and not her, but still...)
So, Texas is one of Siracusa's families?
To be precise, they were a famiglia that originated in Siracusa, but took root in Columbia.
Such famiglie are hardly unusual over there.
From beginning to end, though, old Salvadore saw himself as a Siracusan, through-and-through.
And the Siracusans in turn rewarded him for his perseverance, always affording him a Siracusan's respect.
The Siracusans...
The second act chronicles some of the most praiseworthy episodes from his storied struggles in Columbia.
Siracusans can be found everywhere in the shadows of Columbia's history, and it is precisely amidst that tide that Salvadore gathered his famiglia.
In truth, the contents of this act are the best known of the three.
Even across various retellings, they're almost always the same.
Accounts of 'the Siracusan in Columbia' can be found in over a dozen versions in any local bookstore, mostly romance novels borrowing the phantom of his name, so to speak.
Perhaps, there may even be a few words of truth among them.
If you're interested in that, I can point you to some of the less-outrageous ones.
Ooh, I'd appreciate that!
Think nothing of it. Now, onto the third act - this is the one that can change most dramatically between versions.
Because... it deals with the fall of the Texas Family?
That's right.
Outsiders only know one thing for certain about the fall of Famiglia Texas, and that is that—
Salvadore was murdered by his own son, Giuseppe, who later declared that Famiglia Texas would break away from Siracusa's famiglia system.
This act angered Signora Sicilia, and incurred her retribution.
In a single night, the Texas name was wiped from existence.
The entire Texas family disappeared... in a single night?!
At least, that's what every Siracusan believes.
As far as what exactly happened during the liquidation of Famiglia Texas, nobody except the parties involved have any way of knowing.
Excuse me... How come there are only scripts for the first two acts here?
That is because the third act is not yet finished.
Each playwright will invariably imagine their own version of how this process unfolded, arriving at completely different developments and outcomes.
In that regard, our playwright seems to have reached an impasse.
That said, the contents of the first two acts are already exceptional on their own merits, so I purchased the work without hesitation.
Mr. Bernardo, is it possible that one of the heirs of the Texas Family survived the chaos?
Like, maybe she... managed to leave Columbia and escape to a different city?
For example... Lungmen?
Lungmen? If I recall, that's where you ladies hail from.
Apparently, Salvadore did not have a particularly amicable relationship with his son.
But he doted on his granddaughter, even sending her back to Siracusa, where she was fostered by Famiglia Saluzzo for several years.
Nobody has seen or heard from her since the liquidation, though.
In fact, nobody can conclusively say if she was even involved in this at all. Some say she's been seen in Columbia, or even along the border of Siracusa.
Therefore, different accounts of Famiglia Texas's story tend to have different endings when it comes to her.
She was said to possess their iconic black hair and orange eyes.
If—and I'm speaking hypothetically here! If the last descendant of the Texas Family is still alive, and has returned to Siracusan soil once again...
What would happen then?
Certainly a thought-provoking hypothetical, one with any number of possible developments. Playwrights tend to build their work around such logic.
Sadly, Signorina Sora, reality does not abide by logic.
Some say that she is the 'guest' of a family by the name of Bellone.
Some say that the Bellones are the most powerful family in the city.
Some say that she...
Some say that she left everything behind to go there, not even giving herself a chance to say goodbye.
I, no, we came all the way here to find her.
Your bravery is commendable.
Since you have been so frank with me, bringing secrets even I have never heard, allow me to be frank with you in turn.
Famiglia Bellone and their guests have indeed frequented this theater, graciously offering their patronage to our humble performances.
Really?!
Call it coincidence, or call it fate. Is that not how all stories unfold?
If that guest truly comes to pay us a visit, then our troupe will be sure to extend her an enthusiastic welcome.
Rumor has it that she is a woman of few words, whose twin swords speak for her.
Her name is... Cellinia Texas.
Stop shoving that plate of thistles in my face, Lavinia.
The seasoning here isn't as bad as you make it out to be. Give it a try.
I'm going to have to pass again.
Are you trying to save room in your stomach for a cold dinner with those smug mafiosi friends of yours back home, who reek of cigar butts?
After all, only the things that happen there have any real 'value.'
No... I just seriously can't stand artichokes.
Leon, I've already lost count of the number of violent incidents that have broken out in this city over the past month.
Just this morning, a citizen nearly died at this very restaurant.
You're a judge. You should know better than anyone else that Siracusa was built on violence.
Should there ever come a day when there aren't any unidentifiable corpses lying in the alleys, then that would be real news.
The victim was an employee of il Ministero dei Lavori Pubblici. Your ministero.
...Who did it?
It would appear that Leontuzzo, son of Don Bellone deigns to inquire about the life and death of a citizen only when it concerns his career.
Your Honor, Lavinia Falcone, I really do not have the energy to clap back at you right now.
We can all feel that this city has been on edge recently... waiting for something big to happen.
Something bigger than the Bellones' ultimate victory?
If we really are allowed to take the reins, I, for one, do not intend to continue tolerating the thugs that prowl in the cover of darkness.
Lavinia, you're familiar with the way I do things.
I sure hope so.
Over a hundred years ago, when Siracusa first turned from a plot of land into a country, its territory consisted of the twenty-two cities controlled by the Dodici Famiglie.
And to this day, that number has never changed.
Just like Siracusa's ironclad 'tradition.'
But finally, after so many years, our ever-immutable Siracusa is about to give birth to a new nomadic city.
A new nomadic city means new opportunities, new ambitions, and new hopes. You're exactly right, we are indeed at victory's doorstep.
Economy, traffic, and route planning. We and our friends have already been spreading our influence through every facet of Volsinii's new urban area.
When that area is formally built into a new city, we will have the right to lead the future of Siracusa. At least, that's what the more optimistic associates of the famiglia believe.
And do you consider yourself one of those optimists?
I very much hope I am.
That said, the old fellas over at Grey Hall haven't reached their final decision yet.
There's no future to look forward to until everyone there has formally let the chips fall.
Are we still looking forward to the same future, Leon?
...Of course.
Alright, it's about time for me to head off.
Before the banquet starts, I have to first pick up a certain... 'friend.'
This whole city is sopping wet.
Sorry to keep you waiting.
I was just looking around.
Celli... Signorina Texas, how do you feel about the way your hometown has changed?
These newfangled streets and alleys? That hardly counts as 'change.'
The way I see it, it's exactly the same as the day I left.
And... this outfit. I don't think it's a very good idea.
Consider it your very first gift from Famiglia Bellone. Hopefully, you'll find that our tailor has not besmirched the former glory of Famiglia Texas. Please, be at ease and accept it.
So if I'm not at ease, can I refuse?
Unfortunately, I'm afraid that is not an option.
Guess I'll have to find a way to ease up then.
So, what's my job here?
It's very simple - you'll join me at tonight's banquet, where I'll proudly announce in front of everyone that I, the son of Don Bellone, have enlisted a bodyguard who's inherited the Texas name.
Then, you'll kill all the overzealous, power-hungry idiots there, and we'll call it a day.
I'd actually be down for that.
Same. If only it were ever that easy.
Alright, Texas. In the two days I've left you in the city, I've been hard at work conditioning my mind.
I've been trying to tell myself that the Texas name is worth a whole lot more than the trouble you're going to bring.
That way, I can gradually come to terms with the terrifyingly dangerous situation I've been dealt.
The Texases are already dead.
Unfortunately, not everyone believes that.
Maybe you're the one misreading how much influence a ruined famiglia can have.
Maybe, but I'm not just talking about Famiglia Texas here. I'm also talking about you.
You once returned to Siracusa from Columbia, only to leave again.
The way I heard it, every famiglia involved in the 'liquidation' tried to kill you on your way out, but every last one of them failed.
They were all made to pay a heavy price, and it was all sparked by a single woman—you.
I just work for Penguin Logistics. I do deliveries.
If you've already made your assessment of how much you're worth to Siracusa today, then let me assure you that—
I know at least as much as you do.
First things first, Signorina. You must have some idea of what's going on in Volsinii, right?
...A new nomadic city.
Precisely.
And regardless of whether you acknowledge it or not, this plan is closely tied to your family name.
Or should I say... it was brought on by the fall of Famiglia Texas.
The collapse of the Texases dealt a mortal blow to the authority of Columbia's famiglie, and they had no choice but to return to Siracusa's embrace.
They brought back Columbian technology as the price for regaining our trust. Finally, Siracusa had acquired the ability to build a new city of its own.
However, the most pressing issue quickly became who would have the rights to own it, and the power struggle within Grey Hall began the moment the plan to build the city was completed five years ago.
Today, that power struggle is finally drawing to a close.
As one of the oldest famiglie, we, the Bellones, have the opportunity to seize victory.
Volsinii's backup core is already being retrofitted to serve as the core of the new city, and new plates are steadily being built.
In a year's time, perhaps even less, it will be completed.
And your mission... is to help Famiglia Bellone turn this opportunity into a decisive victory.
Sounds to me like what you needed was just a good enough hitman.
But when the hitman's name is Texas, that might just change everything.
Do you still remember the Rossati name?
Rossati...? Yeah, that rings a bell.
After the Texases were liquidated, the famiglie that had taken root in Columbia lived in constant fear of becoming the next target of Signora Sicilia's wrath.
That's when Famiglia Rossati took the initiative to approach her themselves. They were the ones who brought back the technology to develop nomadic cities.
For a brief moment, the Rossatis were poised to succeed the Texases, even managing to squeeze into one of the Twelve Seats of Grey Hall that no Texas had ever touched.
This seems like one of those boring stories that goes nowhere.
Boring, and brutal. Grey Hall has always been like that - open only to the winners.
And the boss of Famiglia Rossati stationed in this city, Signor Wallach, will not be content lapping up the scraps we Bellones leave behind.
And here I was thinking the dour mood around here was because of the rainy season.
I'll be frank with you: Volsinii has not been peaceful these last few months. A few of our friends have found themselves in various kinds of 'accidents.'

Along with them, the famiglie who still remain on the sidelines may end up changing their minds, and there's no guarantee that the famiglie that have already chosen their alliances won't betray them later.
When Signora Sicilia established Grey Hall, she laid down an ironclad rule: 'Do not kill.'
In other words, the famiglie have a lot of leeway to do whatever they want, short of killing one another.
Could've fooled me.
You are still standing in front of me, after all.
Hmph.
And so, you invited me back.
You don't need me to do anything in particular; I just have to get up on the stage you've prepared and announce that I've returned.
And that stage is tonight, correct?
Signorina Texas, are you aware...
...that the fall of the Texases has been adapted into one opera after another, performed across various major theaters?
In those stories, you, Cellinia Texas, are always portrayed as an ignorant child, helpless in the face of your family's collapse.
But right now, I can say for sure that you are anything but.
I truly want to know... What exactly did you experience during that 'liquidation'?
You've returned, Your Honor?
Yes. Is something going on?
Remember how Alessia said she recently met a pretty nice guy?
The one who runs the textile shop?
So you knew after all!
Yes, we've crossed paths a few times back when I was investigating a case. His famiglia...
Yes, Famiglia Gelli! Though they're not a particularly major famiglia, they still control all the textile businesses for a few streets down!
I heard they even have business dealings with some of the famiglie that make their way in and out of Grey Hall!
Yes, we know.
Alessia has been talking us up for ages. She said the guy's going to bring some of his siblings along so we can all have a good time together!
It's been so busy around here lately, I haven't had a chance to cut loose and unwind!
Oh, Your Honor, umm...
You... aren't going, right?
Right... I still have some work to do. Tell them I send my regards.
Okay! Will do!
Oh right, did you get a chance to look through those files?
Which files?
The mass disappearance on Via Como. Family of five. Remember?
I've located a potential witness and am trying to convince him to testify.
But, Judge Angelo declared that case closed as of this afternoon.
He said... He said that family just moved to another city. Then, he added that we still have too many things to do, and shouldn't waste our time and energy on cases like this.
Unless I am misremembering, I am the judge presiding over this case.
The court made the decision internally yesterday. You weren't present for the meeting.
Is that so...?
I get it.
Right, this case is certainly not worth the time and energy. It's only five people after all.
The husband drives a taxi around the city. The wife picks tomatoes on a farm. They have three children, the oldest only thirteen years of age, the youngest still breastfeeding.
The neighbors were reluctant to say anything, and no matter who I asked, they all said that life must move on.
There was no screaming in the night, no crying by the lake, and no traces of blood left on their squeaky-clean floorboards.
Alright then. Life must move on, and they had it coming for crossing a famiglia. Or maybe their luck was so rotten they didn't even notice they were being targeted.
After all, the famiglie always say they would never attack ordinary civilians.
And as for us judges, there are always more important things to attend to, right?
Your Honor, I've always had the greatest respect for you.
To be honest, I have no idea what an ideal judge is supposed to look like.
Even though the Signora modeled our courts after Laterano's, and codified our laws after Columbia's...
None of us have ever seen a real one of Sankta's wandering judges, and Columbia's Code of Law is but an excerpt from a book.
What I would rather believe is that an ideal judge should be someone as determined to uphold justice as you are.
But not everyone has your talents, or your connections with Famiglia Bellone. You don't have to constantly fear for your life.
No, I just—
You can casually grab a light meal with the son of the Don.
And earlier this morning, Judge Angelo received a letter sealed in a black envelope.
...Which famiglia is behind this case?
Does it make a difference, as far as we're concerned?
Do you still remember last month how Judge Starocci was dragged by his 'friends' to 'chat' in a car for an entire afternoon? He hasn't been able to lift his right hand since.
Then there's Judge Borriello... That chubby, always-jovial old lady. How long has it been since she came to work?
You're right, in theory we're supposed to represent that woman's will. But what is that even supposed to be? At least, I can't figure it out.
Courage and persistence are great things to have, of course, but Judge Lavinia, please do not laugh at the wet clothes of us folks who stand beneath the eaves.
After all, you have nothing to lose.
Is that so...?
Perhaps the price I pay... is the right to truly be just.
Oh, the phones here don't ring very often. They usually prefer to come settle things privately.
Or should I say, they look to settle things with us privately.
Hello.
Got it. I'll be there right away.
Your Honor, you know you don't have to take everything so seriously.
I know.
Should I bring back some macaroni for supper? We're going to a restaurant with pretty good reviews.
...Appreciate it.
You don't know the kind of person he is, but I understand him.
Don't you think we place too much faith in our ability to 'understand' someone? Don't you think we're all too easily blinded by the illusions born of emotion and impulse?
Salvadore... He deserves to step out of Columbia's dark alleyways.
My sister, he is a Siracusan, and you know nothing about how he operates. You really think he could help us get rich?
All he's gonna do is get himself burnt - and we're gonna get caught in the middle.
He uses his fists to protect himself, and then law and order to shackle his fists. Is that not enough for you?
Get your head out of the clouds already.
No, brother. Other Siracusans wear their scars and bloodstains like badges of honor.
Only he knows that blood is nothing more than blood.
So, Sora, how's my acting?
Want me to give it to you straight?
You bet I do.
Absolutely terrible! Exusiai, you've got, like, zero talent for this!
Ehh? I feel like I'm pretty emotionally invested though.
Just being emotionally invested doesn't make you a good actor.
But... as far as the script is concerned, you're on the right track! At least you aren't flubbing any of your lines.
How low were your expectations?!
What do you think, Croissant?
Pretty darn good, if ya ask me!
Only thing is, when yer expressin' yer dissatisfaction with the brother, think ya could dial the emotions back a notch?
Hmm, so try to get the point across some other way? I... can give that a try in a moment.
Woah, Croissant, since when were you an expert?
Hey, don't go underestimatin' a gal who sits in on Sora's classes every week. Not like a certain two someones who come and go without a word.
Hey, I'd never leave without saying anything.
Not what I meant...
But at least this proves we'll be able to find her for sure, right?
Damn straight we will.
Quite the unique performance.
Ehh?
Excuse me, but are you...
My name is Benthonexigios.
Oh, hello, Bensomething-or-other...
Feel free call me Ben.
Sure thing, Ben!
Um, excuse me, Mr. Ben, if there's nothing you need from us, I'm afraid I'll have to ask you to leave. We're still in the middle of rehearsal.
Of course there is. I'm currently watching the performance, am I not?
I don't think someone who's walking around eating pizza counts as watching anything.
Am I not allowed to watch the performance while enjoying pizza?
Well, don't theaters usually have rules against that?
That's a rule imposed on people by the theater, not by the play itself.

Norms have a way of making people superficially conflate these things, and hold them with the same degree of reverence, even erecting false pedestals upon which to elevate them. True reverence, however, is much harder to find.
Huh?
Would you like a bite, young lady?
You betcha!
Bleh, the texture's awful!
It's the cheapest street pizza you can find, after all. That said, their sauce is actually quite respectable.
Um... so if you were watching our performance just now, what'd you think of it?
The key takeaway is that there are some differences between the methods of capturing and expressing emotions that you seem to be adept at, versus the demands of opera.
You are clearly used to standing in front of an audience. Or should I say, you're so used to it that it makes it hard for you to stand on stage as an actor.
That is not an indictment of your abilities, but is nevertheless a trial you must overcome.
Woah, I didn't expect you to be a total pro at this.
Think about it. You are the daughter of a wealthy Columbian magnate. He, Salvadore, is a vagrant from Siracusa.
He's different from all the other men you've ever met. He does not care about your status, but values you for who you are.
He is more indolent than anyone else, yet also cleverer.
He seems destined to become don of a famiglia, setting off on a perilous road from which there is no turning back.
Tell me, young lady, is this someone you could love?
Someone... I could love...
No, you are terrified! The more you love him, the more he terrifies you!
You have no chance of keeping him.
Your intuition tells you that you should escape. That he never belonged to you.
You've told yourself countless times that he is just another passerby on the road of life. That even if he slips away, you can find innumerable others who are better.
You know that if you chase after him, your life will become as a landship in an endless desert, unable to see your course.
You don't even know if the decision you made is right or wrong.
But despite all that, you still turn to your relatives and yell—
'Only he knows that blood is nothing more than blood!'
Wooooah.
Oooooh!
So that's the feeling I've been missing this whole time...
Yet you already seem to understand it well, young lady.
Let's give it another run-through, Exusiai!
Lemme get a sip of water first—
Haha, ya ain't gettin' away that easy when Sora's in the zone.
I'll go fetch some drinks for y'all. And some grub while I'm at it.
Mr. Ben, anything ya'd—
Huh? Where'd he go?
Ah, the police are finally here! Over here, ma'am!
I'm not the police. I am Lavinia, a municipal judge for Volsinii.
Siracusa has no police, Signor.
What? No police?
Are you a foreigner? In this country, 'they' are the ones responsible for maintaining law and order on the streets.
Who are 'they'? I'm just visiting for some business discussions, so I don't quite understand.
Hmph. I'm talking about the mafiosi. You'd best steer clear of them.
I... I know that much.
Where's the scene of the crime?
T-Take a look for yourself... Right there, behind the dumpster.
Five people. They couldn't have died more than a day ago. From their outfits, they seem to be from the same famiglia.
Hold on...
What's wrong, ma'am?
I saw them... just this morning.
They attacked a public official earlier. I confronted them about it, but let them go.
But why here of all places... and their leader isn't with them either.
I... I've always heard that Siracusa is a dangerous place.
No, if this was a conflict between famiglie, they would've made sure to clean up the scene.
This doesn't make any sense.
For your own safety, act as if you never saw anything.
I'll handle the rest here.
Y-Yes, ma'am!
Damn it, I need to get out of this godforsaken place this instant...
Signorina Lavinia, I didn't expect you to ever call me first.
Demetri, noticed any flamboyant hitmen around town recently?
Hmm? Huh... You'd have to either be incredibly brave or incredibly stupid to stick your neck out these days.
...Do you need me to throw last month's crime reports in your face?
I think there's been a little misunderstanding here.
Just now, five bodies were found in a back alley on Viale Orvieto. The scene wasn't cleaned up, which suggests it wasn't the work of one of the famiglie.
And just this morning, I saw these same folks attacking a public official.
Huh, now that's new and exciting.
I don't care about the little power struggle between you people, but don't forget that our ironclad rule still stands.
Setting aside recent developments, even in ordinary times the fact still stands that Volsinii is run by Famiglia Bellone, and has a judge as principled as you.
Pulling a stunt like this is tantamount to spitting in our faces, and nobody would dare do something like that.
Unless...
Unless they're Columbian. Is that what you wanted to say?
It could be anyone who doesn't give two shits about the rules we've set.
Just keep me posted if you learn anything.
I don't recall having to answer to you, Your Honor.
Is this not also 'tantamount to spitting in the face of Famiglia Bellone'?
Fine then, I'll tell Leon. Maybe he has enough spare time on his hands.
First, attacks on government officials. Now, hitmen out of nowhere.
Is there really a connection between these?
The humid air creeps inside.
Lavinia's mind races with the details of the latest case as she makes her way back to the courthouse.
She knows full well what looms overhead, but everyone here, including herself, takes it for granted.
At the entrance to the courthouse, Lavinia looks over at her car, and cannot help but let out a sigh.
It has been splashed with paint, and now stands out conspicuously among its neighbors.
A simple gesture of malice.
These acts used to make her feel at a loss for words, but now they trouble her no more than the continuous drizzle.
Of course, there is still the cost of getting the car cleaned.
Sorry.
Be careful out there, signorina.
The rain doesn't seem like it'll let up anytime soon. You might slip if you don't watch your step.
Thanks for the reminder. But you don't seem to have an umbrella either.
I'm not a fan. I rather enjoy the feeling of raindrops on my skin.
Then I hope you also watch your step.
Why are you trying to provoke her?
And what makes you think that's what I'm doing? Ohh, you think you've got me all figured out now, huh?
Forget I said anything.
I was trying to provoke her, though.
She had a familiar scent on her. One I hate.
Suit yourself. But you're the one who wanted us to break into Famiglia Bellone. And I think this is just making us more suspicious.
I need to know exactly what is happening in this city, and why they wanted to bring Texas back.
So what do you reckon we do? Work our way up from hired guns? Rise through the ranks until we become the Bellones' top hitmen?
Well, Gambino'd probably like the sound of that, at least.
That's way too inefficient.
You guys need some bargaining chips, isn't that right?
So where is Gambino?
Already on his way to the middleman, to cash in the bounty.
Hah, look at that. Here in Siracusa, he's a lot better than you at climbing the ranks.
I don't deny that.
After spending so much time in Lungmen, I'm more shocked at how this place hasn't changed a bit. Not used to it at all anymore.
Trust me, you'll get used to it again soon enough. Just like Gambino.
After all, you're a Siracusan.
You really gonna let him plant his roots down here again?
Why not? There's nothing stopping you either. Be loud and proud about it. I certainly wouldn't get in your way.
The more I think about it, the less I understand. What exactly are you trying to accomplish?
I'm just enjoying the rainy season. What, do you not like the rain?
All I know is, Gambino hates the rain.
So, what news did you get your hands on?
Wanna find out?
Sure, say I do. What would you have me do?
You're in luck. I just happen to be in need of a driver.
...*sigh*.
Come on in, signor istigatore.
I came back alive, bartender.
Your skills aren't half bad. But neither you, Gambino, nor that friend of yours who left for Lungmen eight years ago, Capone, are good enough.
Don't start talking like you're some hot shit, cub!
You're just like the rumors said. Completely clueless as to when you should and shouldn't press your luck.
Is that not how the last Sicilians lost the favor of Signora Sicilia?
Look at the ones today. What can they do now, except cling to their last pathetic scrap of glory?
Listen up, Gambino. I don't give a shit what you and Capone came back to Siracusa to do, nor do I care who's backing you behind the scenes.
All I care about is your ability, and the kind of work you can do.
So, what do you think of our work then?
You're not one of us, and you've got guts. Both are good.
So you keep doing good work for me, and I pay you. The better you do, the more you get paid. Simple as that.
You want to see Bellone. Once you've satisfied me, I'll arrange a meeting.
I'm going to do such a good job that you'll regret it.
You know, I don't hate the fact that you Sicilians ooze ruthlessness from your bones.
That's something Siracusans could use more of these days.
So, let's see you try.
When the time comes, you'll realize that I never regret a thing.
The banquet is about to begin, and people have been lining up early to wait outside the door.
Welcome, Mr. Beato.
And you are?
Rubio. We met at the banquet last week.
Ah, I remember now, il Segretario di Sicurezza Alimentare. Rubio. I do apologize for this porous mind of mine.
Seeing as you're here, I'm sure tonight's meal is not to be missed.
Hardly, good sir. But as we are expecting some rather esteemed guests tonight, I did put in a little extra effort.
I see, I see.
Over here, Beato.
I'll be right there. Now, if you'll excuse me.
Who was that?
Rubio, Segretario di Sicurezza Alimentare.
What? He's actually a government official, looking like that? I ignored him when I first came in because I thought he was just a doorman.
Not that you're missing out on much. He might as well be a clown.
Why do you say that?
He's not from one of the famiglie, and after a lifetime of struggling, the best position he could claw himself into was the director of that no-name dipartimento.
And after he landed the position, he went around kissing the asses of various famiglie until he somehow managed to bumble his way into some renown.
Nowadays, his dipartimento pretty much caters all events in Volsinii, large and small.
He doesn't care about his image at all, standing in front of the door to greet the guests like that. I mean, you saw him.
I see. That said though, there is a thing or two we could learn from his sorry mug.
I must congratulate you, Rubio.
Oh my... What might the occasion be?
Word on the street is that young master Leontuzzo will be attending the banquet tonight.
The heir to Famiglia Bellone, the great celebrity by Carracci's side, has never attended a banquet like this before.
This is your big break, isn't it?
Haha... Signor Wallach, you must have been quite pleased with the batch of steaks I sent you the other day.
Otherwise, I'm sure you wouldn't be calling me just to joke around in such a manner.
Hahaha.
Things have changed, Rubio. I may be the boss of Famiglia Rossati in this city and made a bit of a name for myself, but there are still plenty of rules I must abide by.
For example, I have to break the bread of fellowship with a guy like you.
Your support is always appreciated.
It's good that we all treat each other like family. You scratch my back, I scratch yours. If we have any issues, we settle them on the table.
None of us enjoy spending all day fighting and killing each other. It's just, a necessity born of circumstance. Isn't that right?
Indeed. You work too hard these days, Signor Wallach.
Il Ministero dei Trasporti is headed by a Bellone, and he normally keeps his distance from me. Tch, not much I can do to him.
But il Ministro al Commercio is a smart man, a good friend of mine. Naturally, he enjoys the Rossatis' full support.

The host of tonight's banquet, Carracci, Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici, is an unpredictable man, though. Even though he's got Bellone's little pup following him around, he manages to stay on decent terms with everyone.
Rubio, food safety is very important, is it not? I do hope you are also a smart man.
Of course, of course.
The VIP arrived just now. Excuse me, I must be off.
See you.
I know how strong you are, Signorina Texas. Unfortunately, I am not a man who enjoys resorting to violence.
Using force is the least efficient method.
So if you were expecting to leave Siracusa after making a few heads roll on my behalf, I'm afraid you'll be quite disappointed.
Just... tell me what I need to do.
Carracci, Volsinii's Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici.
He is responsible for the construction of the new nomadic city plate. He's also one of our men, and I need you to protect him.
A government official?
I know what you're going to say.
'The government is just the cloth draped over Grey Hall's round table.'
Every associate of every famiglia remembers Signora Sicilia's lament.
There was once a time when we never took stock of the vases and cutlery sitting on our tablecloth.
But right now, it's something we must pay close attention to.
When the Columbians brought new things back with them, what disturbed me most was not their technology, but rather...
How they do things nothing like we do in Siracusa.
They knew that Siracusa's officials were never going to turn their backs on the old guard of the famiglie, so they never required those officials to defer to them in public.
All they needed was for them to turn a blind eye at just the right moment, or whenever it was convenient for them.
And what can we do? Getting rid of those officials won't solve anything.
Try to settle things with them directly? They're not stupid enough to give the game away.
Despite the not-insignificant amount of power and influence we have here, there is nothing we can do about them.
I believe that you, having spent a number of years living in Lungmen, should be more familiar with their way of doing things.
...To the point I almost thought I was back in Lungmen.
Hah. But here in Siracusa, few people even realize how big the shockwaves from the Columbian approach might end up being.
You know, Signorina Texas, when I really dove into the nitty-gritty of this approach, to better understand it, the only thing I felt was...
Admiration.
The weapons they deploy are profits and negotiations, not violence and bloodshed.
So you also studied their methods.
Studied? No... I'll do it better than them.
And Carracci is my trump card.

Recently, there's been a sudden outbreak of assassinations on government officials around the city. Though I haven't confirmed who's behind the attacks yet, I must ensure the safety of Ministro Carracci.
He's an interesting man, and prefers to walk over taking the car. Even though I have people protecting him in secret, I do not feel at ease.
Now, I've already given them a heads up. Go bring him over to the banquet venue.
(The target is crossing the street right ahead. There should be at least seven mafiosi covering him from the shadows.)
(Seems like there won't be any major issues...)
Would you look at that. Didn't I just say something good was about to happen?
Texas, care for some mille-feuille?
Texas.
Salvadore Texas. What am I to think of you? Should I trust you?
You are a Siracusan. They all say that violence and ambition runs in your veins. That you and your famiglia will devour this place... No, all of Columbia.
Is the man I see before my eyes truly you? So refined, so upstanding?
But I've also seen the way you throw a punch, the blood dripping between your fingers until your opponent is not able to utter even a single curse.
Which is the real you, Salvadore?
Might there come a day when you realize that your two sides are as oil and water? Might there come a day when the trail of blood in your wake catches up to you?
And when that day comes, what would you choose?
When that day comes... would you remain by my side?
A brilliant performance, signorina.
Without a doubt, you've surpassed my expectations yet again.
Actually, it's all thanks to someone in the troupe called Mr. Ben. I would never have been able to adjust so quickly without his advice.
Ben?
I'm not sure we have anyone by that name at Teatro di Milano.
Huh?
He was really knowledgeable about theater and gave me some extremely helpful pointers, so I figured he was an important person here.
Sorry, no one comes to mind. That said, there are plenty of enthusiasts around town who are very well-versed in plays. Perhaps he was one of them.
More importantly, I saw something on your resume that had me a bit curious. Would you mind clarifying something for me?
Sure, ask away.
You've been pursuing a pop idol career in Lungmen.
We've been collaborating with that city more and more recently, but our acting industries have often been at odds with each other, due to their different backgrounds.
But even with that caveat in mind, the clips from your audition sent over by Monster Siren Records convinced me that you have some musical talent.
What this tells me, though, is that you have your sights set on a lot more than just Lungmen.
What drives you to work so hard?
Well... a lot of different reasons, I'd say.
But at the start, I didn't have anything complicated in mind. There was just someone I hoped to catch up to.
Oh?
Hehe, it's just like in the script.
Every time she gazed through the window, or out the door, or across the empty streets, her eyes...
Her eyes made me feel like, one day, she would leave both us and Lungmen behind.
'And when that day comes, what would you choose?'
What's the hurry? Don't you think it's a miracle for the two of us to run into each other in Siracusa again?
I'm busy right now.
What a coincidence, so am I.
Let me guess, your job is to protect this man?
What are you—
Eep...!
Some people really need to learn to read the room. Don't you agree, Texas?
Go protect the target. I'll deal with her.
U-Understood!
So, want some mille-feuille?
...I'll take a piece.
Haha, now that's more like it!
So, Texas. Whoops, now that we're in Siracusa, I should be calling you Cellinia, right?
I'd rather you didn't.
Fine by me. Honestly, I find it easier that way, too. Force of habit can sure be a scary thing, can't it?
Living a leisurely life in Lungmen, occasionally bumping into each other at Rhodes Island, maybe even going on some missions together...
Makes me feel like my swords are gonna go rusty.
This ever-familiar rain is what makes me feel alive.
Swords rust in the rain.
In Siracusa, the rain washes away the blood.
Only people like you - who walk around town with your swords out but no intention to use them - need to worry about rust.
Why are you here?
It's simple, really. Because you are.
Hey, Texas, how long has it been since you were last in Siracusa?
Probably about ten years now?
Seven years and five months.
For more than seven years, the prodigal daughter of Famiglia Texas has not set foot in Siracusa.
I still remember how your grandfather sent you back here to serve as a hostage. You stayed with Famiglia Saluzzo for a while...
Those days didn't last long, but we sure got along great, didn't we?
If that's your idea of 'getting along.'
Even though you're a Columbian, you managed to win the acknowledgment of all of Siracusa's famiglie.
Though, it was truly a shame what happened at the end.
If you ask me, you ought to stay a while this time, and take a good look around.
I'm going back as soon as I finish my work here.
Oh. Going back, you say?
Now that you mention it, there's something I've always been curious about, Texas.
You betrayed the Texases and decided to become a lone wolf.
You even abandoned everything the Texases left behind in Columbia. So why do you still go by the name Texas in Lungmen?
That's none of your business.
Lappland.
I don't plan on staying in Siracusa.
I will go back to Lungmen. There are people waiting for me there.
Okay then. I'll help you.
I don't need your help.
Surely you don't think this is a fight you can take all on your own?
Didn't you find some buddies over in Lungmen? Didn't your time at Rhodes Island teach you that it's okay to rely on others?
Siracusa is no place for tourists.
If Rhodes Island ever plans to set up a branch office here, I would urge them to reconsider.
But most of all, I can't trust you, specifically.
Ah yes, trust.
Texas, you know my family name, right?
Saluzzo.
That's right. And we Saluzzos don't place our bets lightly.
You don't need to trust me, but you do need our backing, isn't that right?
Or do you really intend to let the Bellones drag you around by the leash?
With a deafening boom, the sound of an explosion suddenly tore through the air from not too far away. The flash and flame effortlessly engulfed the government official by the name of Carracci.
A car bomb?!
Oh? Now that's a classic.
S-Signor Ministro!
The banquet venue is under attack! All nearby famiglia associates, hurry and back us up!
Damnit!
Simultaneous attacks on two fronts? Now that's interesting!
Who would have the guts to pull that off, Texas?
Show yourself.
...I knew it.
What should I do if I want to go back to Lungmen?
Ever since Texas took her first step back in this city, that question has always been on her mind.
Going by her agreement with Zaaro, the easiest way would be to obey Famiglia Bellone's every command.
But right now, Texas can already vaguely feel that—
The events unfolding in this city are not so simple.
Talk. Who sent you?
You don't need to know.
But she is not worried.
She understands Siracusa well, and knows that there is no end to these things.
She simply feels a twinge of emptiness, one that even she never expected to feel. Because—
No one will come to her aid in this fight.
And after it's over, no one will ask her if she's hurt, or drag her off to some party.
For her, this emptiness has long since been second nature.
So much so that even after leaving Lungmen for such a brief period of time, everything about that city already seems so distant and nostalgic.

lines.st: 'Out of my way.
Don't stop me from going back home.'

lines.x: But Texas.
The more you want to go back, the more you'll have to cut your ties.
It'll just make you sink deeper and deeper.
What I want to know is, when you finally realize that you're completely covered in mud, what will you do then?
Good to see you again, Your Honor.
Oh, Danbrown, you're here. Looks like I'm in luck.
You washed your car just last week, didn't you?
Nothing I can do about it. After all, it's my job to be hated.
Don't worry, I'll get it nice and clean again for you.
Why don't you take a look around while you wait?
That's alright, I'll just wait here.
Your Honor, I heard that judges have it pretty good.
They drive fancy cars, show up on TV, and even the mayor has to be extra-polite around them.
After all, they represent Signora Sicilia's will.
I reckon I don't see any other judges like you, Signorina Lavinia, who get paint thrown on their car every other day.
You're probably right.
I don't know about the fancy cars or TV appearances, but I could sure use a membership card to your store right about now.
Sorry, our humble little shop doesn't offer VIP services.
You look like you're in a better mood than before though. Did that one famiglia stop giving you trouble?
All thanks to you, Your Honor.
I ran into them on the street yesterday, and they all steered clear of me.
Never thought there could be a judge as fair as you in Siracusa.
Fair, huh... They're just scared of the people behind me, that's all.
You say that, but even if you're backed by Famiglia Bellone, you still doled out punishment to a famiglia well-connected with the Bellones.
In Siracusa, Signora Sicilia is justice itself. I don't know how many of the judges representing the interests of her old famiglie actually bother upholding any real justice.
But deep down in my heart, I know you're the embodiment of justice.
That isn't actually a compliment.
Oh right, I almost forgot. You said earlier that you were having trouble sleeping. Did you go see a doctor?
I did. The doc said I didn't have anything wrong with me physically.
Just some mental stress.
Oh, is something troubling you?
Troubling me...? I wouldn't go that far.
Honestly, this job is basically just a way for me to kill time.
I'm not exactly rich, but I'm not desperate for cash either.
I don't know what I'm supposed to be doing, Your Honor. I just don't know.
How do you usually like to pass the time?
It's me.
What?! Carracci was...
I understand. I'll be right there.
Sorry, Danbrown. Let's chat some other time.
As for your question just now—
I'd recommend giving the theater a shot. I often head down to watch an opera if I have the time to spare.
Danbrown exits the store.
He watches as the judge he deeply respects drive off into the rain, quickly disappearing from sight.
'She's always so busy,' he thinks to himself, drying his rain-beaded gloves on his overalls.
But nothing she does will end up mattering, will it?
Or rather, the fact that she still holds onto hope is precisely the reason why she'll be disappointed again and again.
Things weren't this complicated in the good old days. He looks at the rain falling outside the store, and suddenly remembers an afternoon many years ago.
I hope it rains a little longer, so I won't have to sweep out the garbage in the morning.
Get down!
Eek—!
Damn... Thought you were a pampered little princeling, but turns out you're pretty tough to deal with.
Tch, they're no slouches. Stand back, Rubio!
I'm clearly the one they're after!
I, I know!
Eek!
Tch, there's more of 'em over there too!
Out of my way!
Slippery bastard.
What?! Isn't that Penguin Logistics'—
Hm?
Get down.
Ah, okay!
You dead, Gambino?
Like I'd kick the bucket so easily. Tch, never expected the real master to show up here.
Cut the crap.
The mission's botched. Let's split while we still can.
I think I remember those two...
*cough* *cough*, *cough* *cough*...
Is everyone... gone now?
You alright?
I... think so...
You're wounded. Don't move, it's easy to bleed out.
I've already called for backup. They'll be here soon.
I'll be fine, but how...
How many people would've known about today's banquet?
Are you suggesting...?
You're right. Such a large-scale, coordinated attack must've been planned for a while.
But my identity is public knowledge at this point, and it's not exactly hard to track me down.
In any case, I'm glad you arrived in time.
Carracci is dead.
What did you say?!
What?! You've gotta be shitting me...
