It never made any sense to me before. I fed him plenty of meat, gave him a nice den. Why wasn't he happy?
Think he might've been vegetarian?
Maybe. Maybe it never knew how bad meat actually was until it tried a full-course meal.
It's better to not eat at all... than to eat something bad.
Oh?
Your car's done.
You're pretty good at your job.
Practice makes perfect.
Heh. You never fail to get it done just right.
But, Danbrown, nothing lasts forever.
It's time for you to change your tastes.
...I'll think about it.
Think carefully. Once you've figured it out, you're always welcome to come back.
Are you the backup bassist?
Yes.
Oh, um... Is that your natural hair color?
Yes.
That's special. Usually, you have to dye your hair to work as a bassist. I've heard only those with Famiglia Texas blood have that eye and hair color.
And, now that I'm seeing you up close, your eyebrows... look really similar to Salvadore's. Are you his bastard or something?
No, I have nothing to do with Texas.
All sorts of coincidences out there, huh?
Okay, take a seat over there. Your foundation's really good, so I should be able to get you squared away in no time.
Theater Box
Pardon me, are you satisfied with your seating?
Yes, I've got a fine view.
Excellent, excellent.
If there's anything you need, please don't hesitate to call for me.
Let's head down.
You're not going to stay and watch?
You know I've never been a fan of this sort of stuff. How about I grab you a nice drink?
I'd like—
Brandy, I know.
Sorry I'm late!
No worries. Everyone's still getting ready.
Go ahead and take a seat. I'll do your makeup first.
Okay.
What's wrong? You don't look too well. Are you getting stage fright?
No, I'm fine.
Don't worry, Sora. You've been working so hard, everyone's gonna love you for sure!
I hope so.
Oh right, did you know...?
What's wrong?
Apparently, the bassist got sick and couldn't make it to the performance.
Huh? What are you going to do then?
It's no big deal. The director was able to find a stand-in. She's getting her makeup done in another dressing room right now.
A stand-in... Are you sure that's going to work?
Just trust the director's judgment, alright?
Speaking of the director, he didn't come tonight?
He just dropped by to give some direction before leaving again. I think he's meeting with a guest.
Important people are always so busy, huh?
But it's okay, Sora. You just need to play your part, that's all.
Yes.
Oh, if it isn't Judge Lavinia!
I haven't seen you here for a while. Have you come to watch tonight's opera?
No, I'm just passing by.
That's too bad. But, did you know we're putting on the highly-anticipated 'La Morte di Texas'? I'm sure you'll love it.
Sorry, I'm not in the mood.
That's alright then. I look forward to your next visit.
...Huh?
(Danbrown? What's HE doing here?)
(Did he really... take my advice and come to the theater to unwind?)
(But, today's performance is...)
Wait a second.
Oh, did you change your mind?
That's right.
One ticket please.
Theater Box
Don Bernardo, someone's here to see you.
Oh?
It's Rubio, Segretario di Sicurezza Alimentare.
Let him in.
Of course.
Don Bernardo—
You can just call me 'director' here.
Ah, yes, yes. It's an honor to be here, Director.
Take a seat, Signor Segretario.
Let's enjoy the show first, then we'll talk.
Signore e signori, thank you for being here tonight. You are about to see another spectacular performance brought to you by Compagnia dell'alba.
'La Morte di Texas,' Act II, written by Signorina Caterina.
Every Siracusan already knows the story of Salvadore, but how many truly know the details?
Signora Caterina, who has a deep understanding of Famiglia Texas, provides us with her frank yet intricate perspective.
Let us delve deeper into the life of Salvadore!
(Inhales deeply, then exhales.)
Break a leg, Sora!
Right, time to go!
Phew... Finally found them.
Attagirl, Sora scored us seats with a great view.
Great expectations for her, all around.
Excuse me, would you mind letting us through?
A Sankta girl... An unusual sight in Siracusa.
Ehh? You're a Sankta too?
I've been in Volsinii for a while, and this is the first time I've seen one of my people.
Hehe, looks like it's one good thing after another today.
Go right on by, child.
What's wrong?
That's weird... I don't sense any emotions from him. Is he a strayed Sankta?
But, he's unlike any of the other Sankta strays I've seen...
Well, whatever. By the way, am I the only one who thinks way too much of this crowd looks like mobsters?
I hear tell the head of the Rossati Family's here tonight.
Hey, isn't that what you were just talking about?
Yep. But let's just enjoy the show for now.
(Singing) In an era when chaos and opportunity came hand-in-hand; some were swallowed by the tides of the times where others found in it their promised land.
(Singing) Violence and wealth, power and order.
(Singing) Onto Columbian soil steps a Siracusan, bringing with him only scars, hardship, and ambition.
(Signing) And thus begins our tale. Ladies and gentlemen, come and bear witness, to whom this era truly belongs.
That voice...
I see how it is. You're here to bring her back, aren't you?
But do you really think that's going to happen?
Who the hell are you?
Signore e signori, let the show begin!
It really is you, Cellinia.
Huh...?
Texas?!
—Ehh?
For a brief moment, Texas shifts her eyes to one of the boxes.
She knows Bernardo is sitting there, gazing down upon everything.
She feels it in her heart.
Whether coincidence or some form of necessity, in this moment, Sora is both a danger and a safe haven.
Everything depends on her.
The movement in the auditorium and the faint sounds in the distance all prelude the start of the assassination.
She gestures to Sora for her to stay silent, then proceeds to pluck the strings in her hands.
A most wonderful performance has begun.
Back up, this one's tricky.
Don't fight her head on.
Cover the aisles and the stairs leading up to the Donna's box!
Tsk, tsk. Rossati soldiers are pretty capable after all, huh.
Looks like trying to brute force my way through isn't a realistic option.
Hmm...
Haha, silly Texas. It's pretty obvious she's got a recorder in that bass.
Sounds convincing, though, the way she's plucking those strings.
Hm?
Haha, there it is.
If I can't break through the front... I'll just have to make a path!
Don't panic!
Stand your ground!
Gah, aren't you afraid to die?
Guh... Urgh...
How are the others doing?
The aisles and stairs leading to Giovanna's box are all guarded.
They were ready for this.
Leave it to the Rossatis...
But, we've never been ones to underestimate Giovanna.
Throw more soldiers at them. We're not letting Giovanna out today.
You might not have underestimated Giovanna, but don't you think you underestimated me?
What—
You're... Wallach.
Heh, had I known the show today would've been so exciting, I wouldn't have gone to buy a drink for the Donna.
If Signora Giovanna appreciates a fine wine, I happen to have a selection with me.
No thanks.
The Donna enjoys many things about Siracusa, but the wine isn't one.
Siracusan wines are too weak, same as you people swing your weapons. Doesn't make for a good time.
Maybe you just haven't tried the best Siracusa has to offer.
So, isn't this a great opportunity?
Do you know how long I've been waiting for this?
Bellones and Saluzzos, let's find out what Siracusa's finest wine really tastes like.
Trying to get up this way?
Stop her!
Who said I was going up?
What? Explosives?!
Get outta there!
Woah, the seats over yonder just collapsed!
Is that... Lappland?!
Look over there!
Urgh...
I-Is it another famiglie gang war...?
Move.
Sh-She had weapons in her instrument?!
Texas!
Texas says nothing. She only shoots her a glance, parting her lips for a second before rushing up the stairs.
Sora reads those lips.
She said: 'Wait for me.'
Aaaaaahhh—! Run! Run for it!
What'cha think we oughta do, Exusiai? Sora's in danger!
But—
S-Someone, save me—
Get down!
Woah, you're pretty tough for an old guy.
Do I look that old to you?
Uhh... Well, I guess I wouldn't go that far.
Looks like today's performance is not 'La Morte di Texas,' but rather 'La Morte di Rossati.'
Find somewhere to hide, children.
That sounds nice, but our friend is still up there on stage.
It's a gat-danged hurricane all 'round the thing! We can't just go hoppin' on into that!
Let's go backstage then!
Okay!
C'mon, outta my way!
Huh?
Go, brave children.
Thanks, old man!
I'll buy you a slice later!
Thank ya kindly!
Haha, kids these days are so full of pep.
What's your plan now, Giovanna?
Donna Giovanna, we've got everything locked down. Let's get you out, while we still can.
Why?
It's a rare opportunity to watch a wonderful show from a fantastic seat. I'm going to have to savor this.
Sora, you really are her true friend, aren't you?
Now, how are you going to get up here, Cellinia?
Even if you find a shortcut, getting to me won't be easy.
But—
If I were to run away right now, do you think the Bellones and Saluzzos would just give up and call it quits?
This is a war now, my dear.
The Bellones and Saluzzos have decided to Signora Sicilia's ban on violence, and started a gang war.
Spread the word. There's only one thing we can do now: fight to the end.
Of course.
Nngh...
Looks like 'fine Siracusan wine' was nothing special after all.
I wouldn't gloat just yet... Columbian scum...
What—
Yes, I know. You Siracusans think the Columbian famiglie are just rowdy upstarts who haven't paid our dues.
And yeah, there are a whole lot of incompetent idiots over in Columbia.
But what about you?
Can you even sniff out the scent of blood under your so-called 'guns and order?'
We've never looked down on Siracusa.
But from what I've seen today, you don't even deserve my attention.
Wallach, we've got a situation at the theater!
Someone blew the seats, and Cellinia is heading straight for the third floor.
You've got five seconds for your last words.
Last words?
Heh, what's there to say?
If you're really gonna do it, then tell Leontuzzo that I'm sorry.
Sure.
What—
Leon?!
You...
I'll handle this my way, Demetri.
Signor Leontuzzo. Here I thought we could get along.
You just lost your last chance for that.
Well, well, well. Turns out the heir to a great famiglia just oozes charisma after all.
You've made a fool of me, and still you tell me I had a chance to make things right.
What's done is done.
I'll make you a bet, Wallach.
You're a betting man?
Hahahaha! Signor Leon, mio amico, we're not in a dining hall or one of our offices.
We're in a war, and only one of us walking out of this alive.
Trust me, you'll love this one.
Ugh, these Rossatis are a pain in the ass.
Your Honor? We never heard you were joining us on this one.
And anyway, this ain't something you can just walk into.
I'm not here to stop you. I'm not interested.
I'm looking for someone.
Who?
We got one helluva mess here. Not gonna be easy to find anybody.
All I need from you is to stay out of my way.
But...
Even if I die here, that's none of Famiglia Bellone's business.
Suit yourself then.
There you are.
Hold it.
I work here.
Uh huh. We got rid of all the workers.
Grr, what is he doing here?
Look out, Dan—
Blood splatters, and a few drops splash across Lavinia's face.
Signorina Lavinia.
What are you doing here?
The 'path' opened by Lappland is doing its job. Only a short distance separates her from her old friend.
She lets out a soft sigh.
Chaos swirls all around her, but she knows what to do.
I knew you'd come, Cellinia.
What a look in your eyes there. It's like I'm the prey.
Let's see what the Last Texas can do!
Signor, Director... is this chaos... part of the show?
Chaos?
In my opinion, Segretario Rubio, everything is in perfect order.
What comes next will be even better.
...I see.
You lead one of the most prestigious troupes in Volsinii, and you've put on a play unlike any other.
We do have an intermission coming up, though. You may tell me what it is you want from me.
What I want... isn't very complicated.
With Carracci's death, the seat of Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici is now vacant.
You must be thinking about who the most suitable candidate is.
I take it you've decided you're that candidate?
I don't think there's anyone else better suited than me.
*cough* *cough*, *cough*, *cough*...
Wow... There's still mercy in you, at a time like this...
The Saluzzo Psycho isn't gonna finish me off?
I'm not interested in a pointless fight with some punk who was never taking it seriously to begin with.
...Hah.
What are you waiting for? What do you want?
I made a bet with someone. And I hope I lose.
Wallach looks in the direction Texas went. No one can stop her from assassinating Giovanna.
Except Giovanna herself.
There is nobody else in the empty room.
Texas immediately realizes what's happening, but it's too late. A knife is already up against her neck.
Since I came to Siracusa, I've been thinking of all the different ways someone might try to kill me.
But I never once thought it might be you.
And I never imagined you'd let your guard down, at a time like this.
Did you think that when I saw you'd, I'd give you a big hug and cry 'thank goodness, Cellinia, you're still alive?'
And then we'd sit down and share a drink and catch up on everything that's happened over the last few years?
I'm here to kill you.
Great.
With that word, Giovanna drops the knife in her hand.
She wears a radiant smile on her face, her expression full of longing, as if the murderous intent from a moment ago was but a light-hearted joke.
Long time no see, Cellinia.
Long time no see, Giovanna.
Sora, are you alright?
Yes, I'm fine.
Are you going after Texas?
When I saw her a minute ago, she told me to wait for her.
Let's hunker down until this mess blows over. It's total mayhem out there.
If you don't want to lose her, you're gonna wanna go after her.
Lappland...
I feel like it's the same with Lappland.
For people like her, there's no point trying to guess at what they're thinking. It's better to just accept what they say at face value.
Only if every word they say is true can you start to figure if they're ultimately trying to weave truths into a lie.
Why's that?
Well, do you know who it is waiting in that room?
Giovanna Rossati.
If there's anyone who could actually get Texas to come back to Siracusa...
It ain't me, or Bernardo. And it sure as hell isn't Signora Sicilia.
It's her, Giovanna.
Cellinia, you're a lot taller than you used to be.
Makes sense, doesn't it? It's been years.
By the way, I met some of your friends from Lungmen. Your name came up a few times, and from what they said, I can tell they're worried about you.
...I didn't think they'd come here.
You're nothing like them, at all. I never thought you'd make such lively, outgoing friends with that personality of yours.
You don't count?
If you really thought I was your friend, you wouldn't have gone so long without writing me.
Alright. I know you must be hiding something else.
What about you?
Me?
Nothing much.
The liquidation came too suddenly. None of Columbia's famiglie could sleep at night. I couldn't bear to see that any longer, so I stood up to take charge of the situation.
I opened up talks with Signora Sicilia, and we basically managed to sort things out.
That's all there is to it.
You make it sound so easy.
Honestly, I should be complaining to you.
But it's been too long since I've had anyone to confide in.
So that's why you poured your heart and soul into your scripts.
Haha, could you tell? 'La Morte di Texas' my magnum opus.
There were some details in it that only you'd know.
I guess that means my memory's pretty good then.
But, I could never decide on what to do with Act III, with your 'death.'
I guess there's no need to think about it anymore.
Hah.

Actually, becoming a playwright had another purpose. It's an open secret that Bernardo passed responsibility for the famiglia to his son, while he went on to become the artistic director of his troupe, Compagnia dell'alba.
I wanted a chance to meet him. You know, there aren't many mafiosi who would do something like that.
But the one who ended up shattering all expectations, was him.
I guess I should've expected it after all, from the man who built Famiglia Bellone into what it is in just a single generation.
By the looks of things, he really wants to challenge Signora Sicilia.
You don't seem worried about it.
All I can say is, if Bernardo considers you his trump card against the Rossatis, he's counting his fowlbeasts before they've hatched.
You were right to come to me, Cellinia.
No matter what's going on between you and the Bellones, I can help you.
Join me.
The two of us, together, will make a difference in Siracusa.
And not just because you're Salvadore's granddaughter.
Because I know you, Cellinia. You can make a difference.
We'll start over together, Cellinia.
Don't you want to know how I managed to survive?
Giovanna, it was no fluke that I survived the liquidation.
So you're here, standing on this stage, performing this play... How much do you know about what happened seven years ago?
Seven years ago... Texas's father, Giuseppe, murdered his own father, Salvadore, and declared that the Texases would no longer operate under Siracusa's control.
That upset Signora Sicilia, who sent an elite force from the Twelve Families to Columbia to 'liquidate' the Texas Family.
You were part of that, weren't you?
Look who's such a smarty.
At the time, Cellinia Texas was a star-studded name. She was Columbian, but even the Siracusans had nothing but wonderful things to say about her.
This whole time... you've been trying to beat Texas?
Beat her?
No, no, no.
Back when the Saluzzos were looking after her, we were pretty competitive.
Sometimes I'd win our little contests, sometimes I'd lose.
I did want to beat her, but that had nothing to do with what happened later.
Then...
You certainly do care deeply for her.
Unfortunately, that has nothing to do with the plot of the story either.
At least tell me what happened.
I joined in the liquidation.
I was fantasizing about how she would fight me to the death for the sake of her famiglia. It got my heart pounding and my blood racing.
But, nothing could've prepared me for what I saw that day.
You're a hitman too, aren't you, Danbrown?
I am.
Signorina Lavinia, seven years ago, I was part of the team that carried out the liquidation of Famiglia Texas.
I arrived in the city ahead of time, took up a job at a carwash, and secretly observed the Texases' every move.
Then, the team from Siracusa arrived.
And a group of Columbians found me.
Columbian mafiosi are pretty careless. Real full of themselves.
I could've dealt with them easy enough, if it wasn't for that one girl.
I knew her. She was a Texas, but everyone said she was like a Siracusan.
...Cellinia.
When I see you, I'm sometimes reminded of her.
Was she as stupid as me, rambling on about ideals with a murderer?
Signorina Lavinia.
I never meant to make fun of your ideals. It's just... I can barely believe it.
Hearing you talk about that stuff... it's like listening to a real pretty fairy tale.
To me, your ideals are so pretty, they're a kind of shortcut to get me a good night's sleep.
That day, I was ready to fight for my life.
I was convinced she would see right through me.
But...
She let me walk right by her, just like this.
The audience has scattered in every direction. The battle between murderers has moved on.
The theater has emptied out.
As Lappland speaks, she takes light dance steps out onto the empty stage.
The associates of Famiglia Texas were already pretty much wiped out.
But, what about Cellinia?
She was the only one missing.
We couldn't let anyone live to talk about what happened, especially not the eldest daughter. So, we all went to track her down.
And that was when chaos broke out.
The Texas mansion went up in flames, burning away the famiglia's very existence.
And the arsonist... was standing there at the front door.
Right in front of me.
She slowly turned and walked towards me.
At that moment, I understood.
She decided to stand by and watch the end of her own famiglia.
But who could blame her?
Her father murdered her grandfather, and didn't want to be an accomplice to that.
Only she had the right to blame herself.
But, there's more to it than that.
When I looked into her eyes, what I saw was—
Exhaustion.
Cellinia's story... is no secret to Famiglia Saluzzo, or to me.
After all, the one who led the Saluzzo team at the time was none other than Signorina Lappland.
She got kicked out because she went out on her own to hunt down Cellinia. Her men got caught up in a counterattack, and a lot of them died.
I won't run away, nor will I avert my eyes.
For so long, she has told herself as much.
But this time, her words fail her.
Look at the bodies on the street, Lavinia. Listen to the shouts and cries in the distance.
What can you do?
The only advice I can give you is the same I gave to Cellinia.
Run.
The farther, the better.
Even if your past is going to catch up to you, you'll at least enjoy a moment of peace.
Otherwise— you will die.
But, I...
I still don't want to.
Her past kicked down her door.
It might've been against her will at first, but she was forced out only to find nothing changed—
Maybe she'll decide the things she ran away from weren't actually all that bad.
Her life in Lungmen... might've been a slightly extended vacation.
And now, the one most intimately connected with her family and her past... is standing right in front of her.
That's what you wanted to tell me, right?
Yeah.
If that's the case... I will respect her decision.
Even though you're her best friend?
Because I'm her best friend.
Darn tootin'.
Unless— the pizza in Siracusa becomes inedible.
My grandfather was a sentimental man. He sent me to Siracusa, hoping I would remember my roots.
I lived there for a while.
People there told me all the time that I fit in, like a Siracusan.
But for me, I was just doing what I'd always been doing.
The way I lived my life in Columbia was no different than the way I lived in Siracusa.
And what I saw in Siracusa was no different from what I saw in Columbia.
You might know the story of Famiglia Texas like the back of your hand, but I saw it with my own eyes.
Life isn't a play where every move is sprinkled with a little heroism or villainy. What happened was just an undignified slaughter.
My father bought a counterfeit gun from Blacksteel. When he pulled the trigger, it exploded and did a number on him.
My grandfather fell to the ground. My father drew his knife with his injured hand, but the first stab went wide.
I heard everything from the doorway. Hoarse curses, groans of pain...
The commotion lasted for a long time before it finally ended.
In that moment, I was exhausted. Exhausted with everything.
So I decided to leave.
Are you telling me that Lungmen is your home now?
I don't know. I can't say for sure.
But, I at least know I had a pretty good time there.
You're just like you were back then. Blunt, and straight to the point.
Looks like I'm the one getting ahead of myself.
Here I was thinking you came to ask for my help.
Or at least, you'd want to catch up with me.
But in the end, you're here to tell me... that we can never go back—
You know, Cellinia, that's awfully heartless of you.
I was shocked when I heard you were still alive.
But you went ahead and stamped out my hope, all over again.
I'm sorry, Giovanna.
I don't want to be Siracusan, or Columbian.
I just want to be Cellinia Texas.
This is your last chance.
If you walk through those doors, you'll be my enemy. The Rossatis' enemy.
You'll be in our way, and I, as Donna Rossati, have a responsibility to eliminate everything that stands in our way.
Don't make me do this.
We're just too sentimental, Giovanna.
The most unremarkable Dipartimento di Sicurezza Alimentare...
And its popular but underappreciated segretario.
At this point, men like Carracci, who were propped up to maintain balance between the major famiglie, are no longer needed.
What you need is a puppet that is safe and useful.
Am I wrong?
Signor Segretario, a truly safe and useful puppet would never refer to himself as such.
I just happen to be a bit more self-aware than others.
Haha. And mightily self-aware you are.
You're right, Rubio. You are the best candidate to replace Carracci.
But, if you're willing to be so frank with me, I might as well share something with you.
I'm listening.
My associates killed Carracci.
You've got guts, and I like talking with men like you.
So I'm willing to give you a chance.
Turn around and leave this room, forget what you just said, and no harm will come to you.
Then, go back home and spend some time with your wife and daughter. Savor the last moments of peace in this city.
Doesn't that mean you'd need a puppet like me all the more?
Hm?
You're after this chaos. What's happening all around us.
You want to overturn the law laid down by Signora Sicilia. But you don't care if she rules, or if we go back to the old ways.
The conflict among famiglie never had anything to do with us commoners.
When something happens, we just wait to see the result.
It makes no difference to the commoners whether Signora Sicilia rules, or you do.
But, the winner will need someone to maintain the facade of order.
As the don of a famiglia, surely you understand this principle—
It isn't impossible for the commoners to survive without the famiglie, but the same cannot be said for the famiglie without the commoners.
You're telling me that you hold the real power?
No. I'm making a self-serving justification for the weak.
A good justification at that.
But it doesn't tell me what you're really after.
Power, Don Bernardo.
Since I was a kid, everyone's looked down on me, because I'm weak. And I look like, you know, this.
The oppression I felt from the people around me was more real than anything the famiglie ever did.
I've suffered that my entire life, waiting for this chance.
You tell me you're one who had Carracci killed? All I can think is: thank goodness I came at the right time.
You may not want to hear this, but if you're going up against Signora Sicilia, it's probably not going to go your way.
But who cares? Doesn't matter to me if you win or lose, in the end—
But power is something Signora Sicilia can't give me. It's gotta be you.
You're an honest man, Rubio. I like making friends with honest men.
Hm?
Don Bernardo... (*whispers*)
Give my order. Tell them, that's enough for today.
Of course.
What just happened?!
That room over there... started shaking?
Looks like she chose you.
Huh?
That earthquake is Giovanna's Arts.
Sounds like they had a falling out.
Look, Texas jumped out the window. Let's go after her!
Right!
Ahh, Texas. If, and I mean if, we were friends back then... could I have kept you in Siracusa?
Hah, fat chance, right?
We would've just slaughtered each other.
Lavinia, don't make me... kill you.
I've got a job to do.
Signorina Lavinia, I pray that you aren't born in Siracusa the next time around.
Just a second.
Who are you?
You're one of Saluzzo's hitmen, right? This operation... is over.
Don Bernardo's orders, just in.
Lavinia, you shouldn't have shown your face here.
That's not up to you.
You've interfered with a Saluzzo operation. That's going to ruin the relationship between our two famiglie.
Haha...
So, why don't you go beg Alberto's forgiveness with my head on a silver platter?
Wait, please. Signor Bernardo... I mean, Don...
I have a presumptuous request.
Hm?
I imagine Signor Leontuzzo will be very busy in the future. It'll unrealistic to expect him to help me the way he did Carracci.
I'd like to ask you for a new assistant.
The judge here would be a suitable pick.
That certainly is one option.
What are you two talking about?
Ah, how impertinent of me, carrying on this conversation without including the subject, Signorina Lavinia.
Don Bernardo has decided that I, Rubio, the humble ex-Segretario di Sicurezza Alimentare, shall take Carracci's place as the next Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici.
And I have to admit, I'm not too experienced with bureaucratic affairs.
So I hope that you, as someone with ample experience, will become my assistant.
(I wonder how Sora and the others are doing...)
What? Why... are you here?
When you get old, you start looking for a place to enjoy the rest of your days.
Found her! She's right over there!
Surround her!
Looks like you insist on getting in my way.
She's still worried about you, after what happened.
As for me, I'm old now, so I spend my days taking care of the youth. It's a hobby.
Stay a while and have a chat with your elder.
Next time.

lines.st: Texas, we'll protect you!

lines.x: Croissant?!
Ohoh, let's have a look-see, who's off fighting all by herself?
Why are you all here?
As far as I can tell, the Siracusan families always fight together. It's your culture.
And Penguin Logistics's culture iiiis—
To party hard?
In this case, it should be—
Don't be afraid to rumble, right?
You got it!
Seriously though, old man. You think you could let this one slide, a favor from one Sankta to another?
Get'cher head on straight, Exusiai.
You do have a point.
Ahh, the rainy season in Siracusa is a long one. It didn't bother me when I was young, but now, the arthritis is such a hassle.
Children, striving for strength is fine, but remember to put your health and safety first.
Eh, yer pullin' my leg.
Thanks a bunch!
By the way, if I ever did want to talk with you, how could I find you?
If you're searching for redemption, I'm afraid there's nothing I can do for you.
But if you're searching for order, you will find me everywhere.
That's enough. Let's get out of here.
Woohoo!
Donna.
What's the situation?
The Bellones have retreated.
Good work.
Where's Cellinia?
Gone.
Gone?
She couldn't kill me, and I couldn't kill her.
Let's head back and prepare. The Bellones aren't going to let this go.
Right.
You've won, Leontuzzo.
Your original plan wasn't to win over Wallach, was it?
But wasn't that the best option, given the circumstances?
Can't deny that. *cough* *cough*
Alright, enough talk. Let's get you in the car.
Leon, you still haven't given up, have you?
Compared to what Lavinia has been through, this setback is nothing.
You're going to do great things.
Maybe one day, you might actually be able to realize your dreams in Siracusa.
And when that time comes, you'll be my right-hand man.
I don't get along with Lavinia.
There are some things better left to her, and others better left for you.
Haha, you cheeky bastard. You're already thinking about that kind of stuff?
Demetri... I'm still angry about what you and my father were plotting.
I don't understand why he insists on continuing, even now.
But, in the end, I'm still a part of this famiglia.
Safe landing!
*gasp*... *gasp*... It feels great to be back on our own turf.
Phew... Siracusans sure are a pain in the ass. I'm totally wiped.
You hanging in there, Sora?
Yes. But my dress got a little torn up.
Well, shoot. That one was awfully expensive, wasn't it?
Ahaha, we'll figure something out.
Alrighty, one bottle per person.
C'mon, knock it off with that stone face.
Just think of it this way: the three of us also happened to be on vacation here.
Then... what's with the show?
Oh... That's, umm... just a way to grow my career~
Plus, I thought it might make finding you a little easier.
I reckon it sure did, lookin' at the way things turned out.
Sure enough.
The atmosphere remains chilly for a while.
But finally, an unavoidable problem emerges.
Where do they go from here?
Texas does not have it in her to ask.
Tex...
Texas, I had an agreement with the director.
Hm?
Now that I've found you, I will stay here and complete my performance contract.
But—
I've spoken with the director a few times. It's a little scary, in retrospect, but I don't think he's the kind of man to hold our lives over you.
You have a point. If he wanted to do something like that, he would've locked us up a long time ago.
Okay, alright, let's quit the jawin'. You keep talkin' and I keep gettin' scareder n' scareder.
We really are here to see you. If it wasn't so dangerous out there, I'd like you to show us around.
Okay... When we're through all this, I'll give you a tour.
Anyways, let's celebrate this Penguin Logistics reunion in Siracusa!
Cheers!
The biggest thing—
Hm?
An unknown number...
I don't remember ever giving out this number...
Hello? Who am I speaking with...?
It seems you're safe and sound, Signorina Sora. That's a relief.
You know what happened with Texas, right?
Don't worry, Signorina.
Your Penguin Logistics friends must be enjoying a joyous reunion.
And I certainly don't want to interrupt that.
But I was just thinking you might have a lot of questions for me.
So, I'll be waiting for you at the theater tomorrow.
Right.
Here's the pizza you ordered.
Grazie.
Is someone sitting here?
Yes.
Great, glad to join you.
I said the seat's taken.
You should go and continue your famiglia's work. Don't bother a commoner like me.
Lavinia...
I'm not blaming you, Leon.
I don't blame Bernardo either.
If there's anyone to blame, it would have to be me.
It's my fault I was born into this country and still cling to these fantasies in my heart. I have no reason to blame either of you.
I'm not trying to reason with you at a time like this.
I know full well that comforting words mean nothing right now.
Even though I'm a victim just like you, it's only natural that you see me as a perpetrator.
All I can tell you is... I still haven't given up.
That's all.
There are too many things going on in the city these days. Do keep an eye out, and stay safe. If you run into any problems, give me a call.
Segretario Rubio.
Are you free right now, Your Honor?
Folks on the street seem mighty jumpy.
What happened yesterday was a pretty big deal.
You really gonna go talk to that director?
Yes. There are some things I need to set straight with him, no matter what.
But—
It'll be fine. Keep doing what you need to do, Croissant.
I'll be there. Don't worry.
In that case, I'll be Croissant's bodyguard for the day!
Thanks. I appreciate it.
If things go south, you give us a holler.
Will do.
Croissant, you're thinking about the whole business with Texas again, aren't you?
Readin' me like a book, huh?
You can say that again.
I'm the same, after all.
I know what Sora is thinking. If Texas really wants to stay, she's going to respect her opinion.
And I agree with that.
But—
I still want my sister to meet my friends.
At least, if there's any way to get Texas out of Siracusa safely, you'd want to find it too, wouldn't you?
Yeah... Reckon I can't hide anything from you.
Probably couldn't hide it from those two either.
Just like we can understand what Sora is thinking, they understand what we're thinking.
But this is still Siracusa... and Texas is tanglin' with some of the biggest shots in the country. I got no clue what I'm even s'posta do.
Oh? I thought you would've already figured it out.
Huh?
Uhh... Speaking of big shots in the country, didn't we meet one of them yesterday?
Come in.
Welcome, Signorina Sora. Signorina Cellinia.
Why are you dragging Sora and the others into this?
Sora's resume coming across my desk was honestly a coincidence.
Of course, it could also be something of an inevitability.
After all, Compagnia dell'alba is a pretty well-known troupe in Siracusa.
But what most fans don't know is that the troupe's director is actually the don of Famiglia Bellone. That, of course, is a secret.
After all, I have handed the famiglia's business in this city to my son, and I'm no backseat driver.
Signorina Cellinia, Signorina Sora, I've been directing this troupe for six years.
I hope the two of you can at least believe that my pursuit and love of the arts has nothing to do with my business.
I too would like to see Signorina Sora shine dazzling-bright onstage, if I could, then let the two of you finish your heartwarming reunion.
But, you don't particularly care if she does get dragged in, do you?
Haha.
Drop the pretense, Bernardo.
No matter how you try to sugarcoat it, even if meeting Sora was just a coincidence, how could I expect you to care about her safety?
Enough jokes.
Lovely, Cellinia. This is the first time I've seen you angry since you got back to Siracusa.
Director, there's something I need to know.
By all means.
Is the agreement between the two of us still valid?
You're talking about—
That even if I found Texas, I'd stay with the troupe and continue performing.
Oh?
And here I thought you came to terminate our contract.
What I can do is totally insignificant in the face of the conflict between families.
It'll be too hard for Texas to protect us in all that chaos.
And I trust that you are a man of your word, at least.
So, to cut to the chase, I'm willing to stay here and be your hostage.
Your bravery never ceases to impress me, signorina.
But there's no need to be nervous.
I didn't call you here to castigate you. In fact, what happened at the theater was meant to be a healthy exchange of pleasantries with the Rossatis.
What you've accomplished has already surpassed my wildest dreams.
And so, I'd like to inform you—
That the contract between us is terminated.
You're free to go.
From now on, Cellinia is no longer under Famiglia Bellone's protection.
Is this bargaining chip enough to satisfy you, my friend?
Satisfy me?
You think I'd forget who brought her back in the first place?
All I want to know is, what was the point of this? From the very beginning?
You should thank my son.
He was the one who convinced me you were someone to cooperate with.
Leon? Hah, I see.
I've always been optimistic about Famiglia Rossati's development.
Cellinia was just the one who built the foundation for our cooperation today.
In other words, even if Leontuzzo didn't come to me, you would have.
Like father, like son. Touching.
Heh. I just hope my son can learn a thing or two from your drive.
Listen, Bernardo, other people might fall for your tricks.
But I won't.
Of course, Wallach. I did these things only to show you who really stands in your way.
Oh? Is my biggest obstacle not sitting right in front of me?
If you think you can get me to betray my donna, keep dreaming.
Perish the thought. No. This is a reminder.
Deep down, you know the truth.
Cellinia's visit has greatly affected Giovanna.
Everyone in Siracusa respects Salvadore, but respect isn't blind obedience.
Salvadore's era ended seven years ago. In this day and age, are you still willing to serve Giovanna to protect his legacy?
You could've soared higher and farther.
The Donna works for the greater good.
Is the greater good she wants the same one you want?
Famiglia Rossati used to dominate Columbia, standing on equal footing with the Texases.
Coming back here to Siracusa, they're pushed around by the other eleven famiglie.
Shouldn't your Rossatis rise above the other famiglie, and drag Signora Sicilia down from Grey Hall?
Stop talking like you know me, old man.
I don't know you, but I know people like you, and I know the look in your eye.
Not a single man alive could ever get comfortable with the stagnant cesspool that Siracusa has become.
You understood that when you saw Cellinia walk out of that room alive, didn't you?
Even if that's true, even if what you say is right.
That's something for our famiglia to figure out.
Aren't old-fashioned famiglie like yours the most reluctant to get their hands dirty?
Are you telling me this is what Siracusan dignity means?
Wrong again, Wallach.
I invited you here to share an opportunity with you.
An opportunity to overthrow the existing order in Siracusa, and establish a new one.
Why the Rossatis?
Sixty years ago, Signora Sicilia brought back two things from Laterano: 'Guns, and order.'
Today, people think a unified decision of the Dodici Famiglie created Grey Hall.
But they forget how Signora Sicilia got the Dodici to sit down together.
Absolute power.
Alberto is too prudent, too analytical. He's been cautious his whole life.
And prudence isn't what I need to take on Signora Sicilia. What I want...
Is a friend who's also looking to overthrow the existing order.
Think about it carefully, Wallach.
This is the best opportunity you're going to get.
Is the Donna here?
Over in that room.
Go and tell her that I'm going to deal with Cellinia by abducting her friends.
Are we seriously going to do that?
What you should be asking instead is—
Is this really what you want the Rossatis to be?
You sure the guy we met yesterday was all that wonderful?
Yeah, I've got a hunch!
Why would such a heavy hitter be hangin' around this town though?
I think it makes sense. If I were in his position, I'd do the same thing!
Hearin' it from you does make it more convincing, somehow.
But how d'ya know he's at the courthouse?
Hmm... Oh, there you are.
Well, do you remember the last thing he said? It gave me the impression he'd normally be in court, not at church.
Right, sir?
Sir?
I'm thinkin'... we should leave him be...
Hrmm... Huh?
Oh, it's you kids.
Hello there!
We came to ask you a few questions.
Hahaha! Good, good. Take a seat. It's never a bad day to talk to the youths.
We got a friend who's all mixed up in a contract with of them families. They got her doin' all kinds of things for 'em.
We've been noodlin' on whether or not there might be a way out for her?
Your friend's surname is Texas, isn't it?
...Yes.
I've heard that Bernardo is a man of his word.
As a don, he must never break his promises.
But even us foreigners can tell all this hubbub is the Bellone family tryin' to rile up Signora Sicilia.
We... ain't about to go stickin' our heads in all this complicated business. And to be honest, it don't got much to do with us, neither.
But we don't want Texas gettin' any payback from Signora Sicilia.
Back then, Sicilia's fury towards Giuseppe for what he had done was just as great as her admiration of Salvadore.
Because it was the most direct challenge to the order she had established.
Sicilia accepts all comers within this order, but she will not allow anyone to upend the entire order.
So what you're sayin' is... There ain't no loopholes in this order, izzat it?
I used to think that, my child.
But there are no absolutes, when it comes to orders established by man.
Just as why me meeting you has nothing to do with Cellinia.
It was just because I happened to meet a fellow Sankta here.
Heehee~
Before Sicilia, the land of Siracusa was not without order.
The only difference is the order that the families believed in came from themselves, and as no two famiglie could ever share the same order, they could only fight.
Sicilia's actions did not completely break this order.
Instead, it unified the orders among different families into a single, universal standard.
All the families were united into one.
And thus, Siracusa became Siracusa.
Now tell me, do you know what this means?
Yer sayin'... Siracusa, before n' after Signora Sicilia... never actually changed?
Clever girl.
The problems that existed in Siracusa in the past still exist today. I, no, we, are all aware of this fact.
But all these problems can be resolved by seeing Sicilia's fold.
Meetin' Signora Sicilia... that's a tall order.
That is one thing I will not help you with.
But, for her, there might be a way.
However—
However?
The price may be too high to pay.
Donna?
Hmm?
You've been staring at that necklace in your hand since last night.
This is a symbol of trust given to me by Signora Sicilia.
For picking up the pieces of Famiglia Texas, and leading the Columbian famiglie back to Siracusa.
Oh, it must be pretty valuable.
Are you gonna use it to help, somehow?
I'm still on the fence.
You said Wallach plans to attack Cellinia's friends?
Yes. They stepped out a while ago, but when they get back, we'll have an ambush in place.
Where are you going?
To buy some chocolate.
Tsk, tsk. Turns out, your guess was right on the money.
Rossati associates have already started to infiltrate the block.
When those ladies from Penguin Logistics get back, I'm afraid they're gonna die a gruesome death.
But, lemme ask you a question, Lappland.
Lay it on me.
I figured the Rossatis would try to rub out Texas.
But you're still a Saluzzo. You really need to send Gambino after that Sankta while you stay here and house-sit for 'em?
I never knew you were to give so much for the sake of friendship.
Am I that type?
Definitely not.
Which is why I want to know, what the hell are you waitin' for?
You plowed that truck right into the courtroom, turned yourself in, and cleared her name in the process. Then you went on and forced the Saluzzos and Bellones together.
I got no clue why the two famiglie actually started working together. But there's gotta be another reason for it, right? You know that by helping the Bellones, you're also helpin' her.
And that's before we talk about how you gave her a hand in yesterday's assassination.
You say you wanna prove to her that Siracusa's a swamp you can't escape.
But it looks to me like you're tryin' to help her escape.
If I need to kill a few of her loved ones, or do something to piss her off, just to prove my point...
All that would show is how paranoid I am. Wouldn't get anywhere with that.
If it's real, if it's a fact, then it'll happen, even if I just stood by and watched.
But actually, even that isn't good enough.
I can't just get out of her way, I have to give her enough help so that in the end, she has to finally admit—
That all her struggling was for nothing.
And doing it like that will bring you some satisfaction?
Dunno. All I do know is there's no other way.
So basically, you don't know what to do either.
Yep. And I never said I did.
And you, Capone, you should be more like Gambino and realize that you'll never get the answer you want from me about the two of you.
You already knew, huh.
He wants to stage his comeback by joining up with the Bellones, but what about you?
You're still standing here. Do you seriously think you're my vassal?
Or, are you hoping there's enough room for you on my path?
Give up that hope, or I'll kill you.
There's only room for one.
What about Texas?
Texas... might just be the path itself.
Hah.
Capone stays silent for a minute before turning and leaving.
Ahh, what a pleasant surprise.
I haven't been here before... but it looks like I'm in the right place.
So this is Penguin Logistics's little stronghold.
Miss Caterina.
Sora... Oh, right, I gave you my number before.
Would it be better if I called you Miss Giovanna?
Caterina will do just fine.
I like the name.
Do all Siracusan mobsters like to work in the performing arts?
Why not? We can't be fighting and killing each other all the time.
Are you all together right now?
Miss Caterina?
Never mind.
Sora, would you mind telling me more about your Penguin Logistics?
Huh?
For example, how it came to be, and how Cellinia joined.
I'm not too clear on the details myself. After all, Penguin Logistics was already a thing by the time Texas saved me.
The boss mentioned a few times how he set up Penguin Logistics after he met Texas.
But when it comes to stuff that happened after I joined, I could talk for days about that.
We've been living together for years.
*sigh*... I'd really love to chat with you more, if we get the chance.
There's a lot I'd like to tell you about Cellinia.
I also want to hear more from you about her life in Lungmen.
If you ever get a chance to drop by Lungmen, we'd be glad to show you around.
Really?
You're not worried about my business?
What matters is you were once Texas's best friend.
Cellinia truly is blessed to have met a woman as kind as you in Lungmen.
If you ask me, she's blessed to have a friend like you in Siracusa who's always thinking of her.
Oh right, Sora.
Yes?
I had a flash of inspiration just now.
Huh?
I'm always thinking about how I want the grand finale to play out.
In my story, I thought she should meet her end just like a Siracusan would.
Surrounded, in a narrow alley, incinerated by a car bomb, so on, so forth.
These little excerpts from reality are so overused that they've turned into their own tropes.
I want to escape the tropes, and in this story, have her become the least Siracusan Siracusan.
I've been trying to find a 'Siracusan' archetype, but haven't been able to define it.
That's the source of my writer's block.
But right now, it's starting to come to me.
There are no Siracusans.
Cellinia Texas finally disappeared in the conflagration, and nobody knew what became of her.
Miss Caterina, are you...?
I'll be back later. I have some presents for you girls.
Wallach, if memory serves me right, you're trying to kidnap Cellinia's friends?
They're not even back yet, and you've gathered a whole mob?
I would have, if you didn't show up.
I'm extremely disappointed, Donna. No, Giovanna.
Were you that impatient with me?
It's not a question of patience. It's just, why should we wait?
Why should we have to sit here and wait for the blessing of some antique?
We followed you to this country, waiting to become masters of this land.
And now, we have an opportunity.
Are you so sure? You're certain victory is within your grasp?
Nope. I'm not even certain I can deal with that antique...
But I know Bellone really wants to bring us into a new age.
And I've got a hankering to make a big bet.
How many are willing to follow you?
Seventy percent.
What about the rest?
I'll convince them.
Or, you could do it for me.
If you decide to change your mind, Giovanna, you're still our boss.
Looks like I brought up some good soldiers after all.
Sorry, Wallach.
Is Texas more important to you than Famiglia Rossati?
I just... don't think this is how things should play out.
The way I see it, some things can only play out like this.
Texas!
What?
Miss Caterina... I mean, Giovanna, might be in danger!
What did you say?
I just called her, and have a really uneasy feeling.
Lappland.
Yo.
What do you want?
I just wanted to give you an update on your old friend. She's somewhere near your little hideout, surrounded by wise guys from her own famiglia.
Texas, we should go help her!
...I know.
Just a sec.
Are you seriously going?
You were all ready to make an enemy of the Rossatis to get out of here.
But now you're going to stay in the swamp just for her?
Think, Texas.
This is your best chance to turn around and walk away from it all.
Sora...
You know, Texas?
This was supposed to be a little job I picked up as a way to go looking for you.
But, I soon came to realize that I'd fallen in love with Siracusa's performing arts scene.
People here have a certain arrogance to them, but they're much better than the materialistic Lungmenite businessmen.
And Exusiai won't stop raving about how she's going to find the best pizza in Siracusa.
As for Croissant... She's always been easygoing.
I just wanted to catch up with you. Maybe we can go back to Lungmen after everyone's had enough fun here.
We'll go back to Lungmen eventually. The four of us, together.
That's right, the four of us, together.
Well, actually, I just had a chat with Miss Giovanna and asked her to take a tour of Lungmen with us. So that'd make five.
...Okay.
A number of people have collapsed in an alley.
A number of wounds have appeared across Giovanna's body.
Everyone, including herself, knows there is no escape for her.
Give it up, Giovanna.
Got anything to drink, Wallach?
Go grab a bottle from the car.
Yessir.
I've thought about it before. If we would've been better off getting into certain businesses back in Columbia.
Originium weapons, designer drugs, military channels...
I've also thought about... if there were certain opportunities that I should've seized.
Getting on good terms with those officials, or even extending an olive branch to the Columbian government.
But in the end, I decided that some things had to be done in moderation.
We're too deeply invested, we're holding too much. I won't claim to be a good person, but at least we can't forget that there are some things we can't do.
You could call it principle, or you could call it stubbornness.
Times have changed, Giovanna.
A new era calls for new norms.
The so-called 'morality and justice' of the old guard should be cast aside.
Even if we don't do it, someone eventually will. And when that time comes, we'll be the ones who get pushed out.
Or you think sitting around, watching Columbia's other famiglie get their mitts on these things is the right way to do it?
What would you do in my position, Wallach?
I'll control it all, new and old. Everything will be at my disposal.
We're not taking the world back to the old ways, we're turning away from them.
Got the booze, Wallach.
Come on. Have one last drink with me.
Fine.
This one's my favorite. You've been waiting a long time for this, haven't you?
It was supposed to celebrate your return.
Hah.
Well, how about that. From here on, you're Don Rossati.
Giovanna.
Do it.
The sound of sharp metal slicing through flesh appears in the alley.
It is quickly drowned out by the sound of the falling rain.
Texas knows she is already too late.
The alley is scarred by traces of battle, and blood spreads out across the ground.
Her gaze goes to the source of the bloody water, where a familiar figure lies fallen.
Her heart quivers.
Giovanna!
We should be able to have a nice discussion over here, Your Honor.
I won't become your accomplice.
Accomplice? That's a rather extreme choice of words.
Shouldn't you be thanking me for saving your life?
Forget it, just treat it as an act of kindness on my part.
The upside about you young folks is that you're go-getters. The downside is you tend to overdo it.
There's nothing I particularly need you to do for me, actually.
I just don't want to see an outstanding young woman like you fall.
You? Really?
I think the Carracci incident has given you some misconceptions about me.
No, I don't think it has.
I happen to care more about what's in front of me, that's all.
'The government is just the cloth draped over Grey Hall's round table.'
Lavinia, everyone in Siracusa knows of Signora Sicilia's lament.
But I'd imagine there are few who understand the source of this lament.
Thirty years ago, there was a group of youths like you who naively believed they could free Siracusa from the famiglie.
But there was no end to the dark clouds that hung over the country, and they couldn't conjure up any support.
So they chose to try to win over a number of famiglie by catering to their interests, trying to fight... the thing they thought they could resist.
As for what happened to them... nobody knows.
Nobody knows?
Try to picture this scenario, Signorina Lavinia.
Your father is a clerk who works for the city government.
One day, you get up, shower, and have breakfast as usual. That's when you realize your father forgot something at home, and your mother asks you to bring it to him.
It's the rainy season, and you're in a bad mood because you had an argument with your father. But then you think to yourself, it's partially your fault, and it would be better to just apologize.
But when you walk into city hall, you feel an unnatural stillness.
The place shouldn't be so quiet at this time of day.
Soon, you see blood seeping from beneath the doors of various offices.
And that's when you realize that streaks of blood are painted all across the floor.
You find that everyone in city hall has gone missing.
What scares you even more is that not a single soul will comment on it, no matter who you ask. Even your own mother is completely silent.
It's as if the disappeared never existed in the first place.
After a while, people begin to appear at city hall again, and a bit after that, the normal hustle and bustle returns.
And you finally realize, that's all there is to it.
That's when Signora Sicilia's saying really starts to spread.
'The government is just the cloth draped over Grey Hall's round table.' And naturally, the tablecloth can be changed at will.
When you've lived through it, you'll understand—
Anger. Of course the anger is there. But what happens after the anger passes?
After anger, comes nothing.
Just as you are now, not knowing who you can even vent your grievance to.
To Don Bernardo? To Famiglia Bellone? Or to Signora Sicilia?
No, of course not. They are all cogs in the existing machine, and you, as the gatekeeper to this machine, know better than anyone else—
It can't be shaken.
And that's why you want to tell me... that all I can do is to cover my eyes and ears like you, pretending to be oblivious to everything that's going on.
Pretending I'm living a semblance of what you could call a life.
Signorina Lavinia, you are even more naive than my daughter.
And as someone who's experienced it all, my advice to you is the same as the last time you came to me—no matter what your ideals are, you must first live on.
And if you're really in that much of a hurry to die, at least find a successor first.
Sometimes, living to pass down your ideals can be even more difficult than dying a merciful death.
Oh right, I'll be taking up my new post in a few days, and Don Bernardo has arranged a speech for me to deliver to the whole city.
There's nothing I need you to do for me, but if you'd like, you are welcome to keep my wife and daughter company.
I do believe that isn't anything that would violate your principles.
Hmm? You're not going with the rest of your friends, my child?
Uhh... I have a somewhat personal question I'd like to ask you.
Oh?
Please continue.
I learned a few things about you back when I was in school. Back then, both you and His Holiness the Pope were seen as promising young talents.
But after Signora Sicilia visited Laterano, you left with her. Didn't even hesitate.
You want to know why I left Laterano with Sicilia?
Not exactly. What I'd like to know is, you must get along very well with Signora Sicilia, right?
That depends on what you mean by 'get along.' But if you mean in terms of understanding, it may be the case that nobody understands her as well as I do.
However, our personalities are actually quite incompatible.
At least these days, I am happier hiding in this city to avoid the headaches she brings me.
Isn't that also part of getting along with someone?
That, I cannot deny.
Then, how were you able to stay on good terms with her for decades?
Hahahaha!
Senior Agenir, I'm serious!
An excellent question indeed, my child.
You certainly ask much more interesting questions than your friend.
But first, there's something I'm curious about.
From the description you just gave me, you seem to get along very well with Texas.
That's right, we're the best partners out there~
You don't doubt your friendship at all.
Not even a little.
In that case— Ah, I see now. The reason behind your question.
Shared empathy allows you to treat people without reservation, but you still have doubts about the process of getting along with those who lack that empathy.
Moreover, it is not deception that you fear, but this doubt itself.
You are ashamed of this doubt when you are reciprocated with nothing but sincerity.
But at the same time, you are also afraid of how you will handle yourself if these doubts prove to be true one day.
Is that the case?
Um, yeah, that's exactly what I was thinking!
I think every Sankta who leaves Laterano will encounter this problem.
When Sicilia came to visit Laterano, I was tasked with serving as her guide.
However, I did not leave Laterano with her because I was persuaded by her ideals, as the rumors claim.
Sicilia was still young at the time, and no matter how talented she was, it was impossible for her to instantly come up with a way to change Siracusa after but a brief stay in Laterano.
Her ideals were puerile and unrealistic, but they happened to resonate with some of my own thoughts at the time.
We discussed and argued our ideas day and night until finally, I decided to go with her to Siracusa to settle them.
Discussing, and arguing...
Yes.
Even after coming to Siracusa, we never settled the arguments between us. As a matter of fact, our views on Siracusa have never quite lined up.
But this does not prevent us from understanding what the other is thinking.
So, the key is discussing and arguing?
Haha, what do you think?
I don't think that's right.
I don't think Texas and I ever had any serious discussions about anything. I'm not even sure when it happened, but we somehow became really good partners.
You're a perceptive one, my child.
In the end, what kept me bound to Sicilia was not ideals, convenience, or even emotions.
But time.
Time?
The passage of time will bridge most gaps and bind people together, regardless of what they think about it.
You'll always be good partners, won't you?
Of course. Always and forever.
Then, all you need to do is keep moving forward.
You will experience many different things, as will she.
Perhaps your stories will individually have very little in common.
But as long as your lives intertwine at some point...
Well, you'll have nothing to worry about.
Oh, you really came, Judge Lavinia!
You must be Segretario Rubio's daughter, yes?
Yeah! I thought my father was just joking around when he said you'd come babysit me!
My friends and I all adore you.
Compared to people like my father, you're like, the only clean person in the government.
Do you not get along well with your father?
Meh, so-so, I guess.
I still like him as a person, because he always makes time for me and plays with me a lot.
But you know, I think people like him, who only care about protecting themselves and not rocking the boat, really should have bigger goals in life.
His old work friends who started out on the same level as him are way up there now.
It took him this long to land a half-decent job, and he doesn't want to do anything more with it.
His old work friends?
Yeah, one of them was Carracci, Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici, who just died not too long ago.
It's a dangerous job.
I know... But, I saw him on TV every day, and wise guys from all the big famiglie treated him really well.
I dunno, I just think it doesn't make sense trying to be a politician if you can't make it to that level.
That might be true.
So, that's why I was really surprised when my father suddenly decided to become the new Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici.
I'm not sure what finally made things click for him... But if he did it sooner, my mother probably wouldn't have divorced him.
Divorced?
Yeah, she thought he was a good-for-nothing, so she left him a few years ago.
But my mother has a temper, so I decided to stay with my father.
Oh, by the way, you know how he got appointed ministro, right? Can you tell me more about it?
Sorry, I actually don't know anything...
Aww, okay then.
Anyway, you can sit anywhere you'd like.
I'm not going anywhere today, but I do want to listen to my father's inaugural speech.
(Segretario Rubio... keeps his room tidier than I would've expected.)
You're surprised too, huh? He probably seems like he'd be a total slob, but he keeps things pretty organized.
Oh right, and he keeps a diary.
Really?
Yeah, lemme think... Right, he keeps it over here.
He only writes in it when he feels like it. It's not an every day thing.
After years of jotting stuff down, he's still got all these blank pages.
Does he not care if you snoop around his things?
It's totally fine. He left it there because he doesn't mind people reading it.
I've flipped through it a few times, but it's all boring stuff. I'd call it like, chronicles of a middle-aged man? Boring, it drags on and on.
You can take a look if you want.

lines.st: March 5th
I ran into Carracci at a restaurant. We had a drink for the first time in a long while.
Reminiscing about old times, I couldn't help but to get teary-eyed.

lines.x: Signor Rubio, this is where you'll be giving your speech.
Please leave me.
But we've got orders to protect you.
Nobody could get in here, right?
Right. Okay.
Hmph. Power, huh.
Now I'll barricade the door with the sofa... This is about as much as I can do.
Okay...
Well, Carracci, I won't keep you waiting much longer. See you soon.
How's it looking?
The doctor says the strike just missed her heart by a few centimeters, so she didn't die instantly.
But, she's still in really bad shape.
Where's Texas?
Umm...
She's been in a stupor ever since she got back yesterday.
She just sits there motionless. Won't even respond when I ask if she wants to eat.
Just give her some time. Croissant and I will guard the hospital, so please take care of her.
No need to worry about me. You two look after yourselves.
*sigh*... So bored.
Oh right, I heard there was supposed to be some speech today?
Maybe I'll give it a listen.
Buongiorno, my dear friends and citizens.
I imagine most of you don't know much about me, and I can't blame you for that.
After all, I came held very minor and unimportant role of Segretario di Sicurezza Alimentare before this.
In fact, I believe that most of our dear citizens, apart from the late Ministro Carracci and the honorable Judge Lavinia, know very little about our government.
I do not know Judge Lavinia very well, but I do have somewhat of a history with the late Ministro Carracci.
I believe it would be appropriate to begin my speech with him, a figure everyone here should already be familiar with.
Carracci and I both joined L'Autorità Garante della Concorrenza e del Mercato around the same time.
He was strong and energetic, taking care of the fieldwork while I handled the paperwork behind the scenes.
He became known within the system for his bold words and actions, and he even dared to challenge prominent famiglie.
To be perfectly honest, it seemed like the guy was indomitable. He was almost taken out a few times by dissatisfied famiglie, but managed to weather the storm or push through the situation.
But at the time, L'Autorità staff saw him as a pain, and nobody wanted to help him.
So the job of reporting to him naturally fell to me, unpopular as I was.
And let me tell you, that job took a whole lot out of me.
But at the same time, it filled me with a sense of accomplishment.
Though I could never be as direct and straightforward as him, I could do what I wanted to do through him vicariously, and that was a reward in itself.
Throughout this process, the two of us became fairly well-acquainted.
Leon, you were working with Carracci for the last two years, and I know you thought highly of him.
Does this account match with what you knew of him?
He certainly was energetic.
He knew exactly how to deal with the famiglie, and that went beyond just being tactful. He knew how to help them under the table while also getting them to serve his interests.
A lot of what he wanted to do would've been blocked by various famiglie, but in his hands, he always managed to get things done, step by step.
I can tell you learned a lot from him.
I just thought there were some similarities in the way I saw things and the way he operated.
The days when problems can be solved with fighting and killing are over, padre mio.
If the famiglie are going to survive, they have to adapt accordingly.
You still don't understand, Leon.
What do I not understand?
You've subconsciously lumped yourself in with Lavinia and Carracci.
But don't ever forget, you are not one of them.
I know.
If you really knew, you wouldn't be standing here.
Unfortunately, you're out of time.
Padre?
Leon, if you get through this alive...
It's what it is.
Even though he was a thorn in the side of many famiglie, his talents were evident to all.
And so, he rose through the ranks long before I did.
In fact, I thought he wouldn't last long after his promotion.
I used to think that, because he was never in a place where too many different interests overlapped, he wouldn't face any serious attempts on his life.
If he kept behaving the same way he always did as he rose through the ranks, sooner or later, he was bound to step on toes that he could not afford to step on.
I was wrong.
I never knew my father could be such a talkative person.
What do you think, Judge Lavinia?
Judge Lavinia?
Lavinia seems completely oblivious to the calls of Rubio's daughter.
She is engrossed in the words of the diary in her hands.

lines.st: May 3rd
Just resolved a small case, but it touched me deeply.
There's a problem that everyone is unaware of. Or, should I say, is turning a blind eye to.
Why, even in the age of Signora Sicilia, does Siracusa have two different sets of rules at once—one internal, and one external?
The brutality and greed of the famiglie have long been documented, but as brutal and greedy as they may be, they have chosen not to control things themselves.
There's a reason for this.
It's actually quite simple.
Population.
The institution of a famiglia is based on bloodline or personal identity.
And this inefficient organization means that the numbers in any given famiglia will never reach the order of magnitude required to manage even a nomadic city.
This means they must rely on a system established by the common citizenry, and they can only seize control of a behemoth such as Siracusa by manipulating this system behind the scenes.
Signora Sicilia was acutely aware of this fact, so after she rose to power, she drew an even more pronounced line between mafiosi and ordinary folk.

And by emphasizing her absolute might and control, she, over the past few decades, has gotten everyone in Siracusa to believe that in Siracusa, 'the existence of two orders, internal and external, is taken for granted.'
To the extent that everyone was forced to forget how the establishment of Siracusa's underground order was only made possible by the consistency of the outward-facing order.
This cannot be called anything other than a manifestation of political wisdom.

lines.x: Rubio, you...
In fact, in our system, there is no shortage of people with the same fundamental intentions as Carracci.
However, for various reasons, they finally chose to submit to these famiglie, even becoming their cronies.
But Carracci was different. Not only did he not give in, he was always looking for ways to fight against them.
And, his efforts paid off.
He did not die as easily as I thought he would, and he even managed to climb higher.
And when we met again at a banquet, he said something that I will never forget—
'The famiglie these days are weak.'
This dude's pretty brave...
Huh?
Umm... Texas, there seems to be something happening outside. Lemme go check it out.
Exusiai.
Huh?
Do you think... we could bring Giovanna back to Lungmen?
Why not?
She seems fun.
But, it doesn't feel like that's what you think. Right?
Texas turns her eyes to the table by the window, upon which a necklace sits.
It was a symbol of trust from Signora Sicilia.
A gift that Giovanna left for her.
In her grandfather's eyes, the Columbian famiglie had soured.
They'd do anything for the sake of profit, getting involved in the lowest businesses, committing murders with no morals at all.
In her father's eyes, the Siracusan famiglie had decayed.
Passing up profit and opportunity, not fighting for their interests, limiting their progress for the sake of supposed morality. All of it was the pinnacle of stupidity.
In Texas's eyes, her father and grandfather were right and wrong.
If... If she is only angry about what happened to Giovanna,
then she could leave just like she did seven years ago, only this time bringing Giovanna with her.
However, when she got news of her grandfather's death, when she turned a blind eye to the carwash worker, when she turned her back on her father's orders to help, the only thought on her mind was—
There's no end to it all.
He showed me how to deal with the famiglie in a comically simple way—
He offered them goods they couldn't refuse. Benefits.
It's not as if the famiglie couldn't take what he had.
But what they always lacked was a great manager. They only knew how to plunder, but never actually learned how to govern.
Relying on his outstanding abilities, he gradually became a celebrity to several prominent famiglie.
And when they fought over who would fill the role of Ministro dei Lavori Pubblici, he became the candidate that everyone was happy with.
Having said all this, I'm sure a lot of you will have questions—
You might think Carracci sounds like a guy who played both sides.
But what I'd like to say is, in a country like Siracusa, a country rooted in evil, it has never been possible for a good person to exist.
To put it mildly, we are all evil.
Should he be saying stuff like that in an inaugural speech?

lines.st: October 15th
I met with that judge, Lavinia.
From the way people talk about her, I imagined someone like Carracci, a righteous person who wasn't willing to throw away her integrity.
Meeting her only confirmed that.
There's no way she's unaware of the futility of what she's doing, in our current landscape.
There's no way she's unaware of the fact that should she ever cross any of the famiglia, her life would be in grave danger.
The irony of all this is, the rights of ordinary people that she strives to protect as a judge mean nothing in the eyes of the famiglie.
They mean so little to them that she has not been snuffed out on her path forward.
Talking to her was torture, because I had to hold back my admiration for her and treat her like I would anyone else.
Carracci once joked that my position made him feel at ease because even if he died, he knew I would be there to pick up the torch.
But... what if I also died?
It's not as if I haven't cultivated and won over a number of youths, but they've never had a leader with any real sway like Carracci.
And undoubtedly, Judge Lavinia would make an ideal successor.
Of course, I can't push my ideas on her.
But, I'm afraid I don't have much time left either.
There is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity in front of me. I don't have time to convey each and every one of my thoughts to her.
I do believe, however, that if my plan goes well, Judge Lavinia will at least be willing to look after my daughter.
Alas... Carracci, my old friend, let me put your death to good use.
If our souls can meet once more, I'll have to apologize to you again.
—But, a noble soul like yours probably won't end up in the same place as mine, haha.

lines.x: Ugh, I knew we should've relocated as soon as we could.
But we're just gonna have to work with this, considering everything that happened yesterday.
Gah, that Sankta's a pain in the ass.
It's just one woman! Surround her!
My trusty partner here is trying to relax, so mind keeping it down?
Ugh, you guys sure love raising hell, don't you?
Oh, if anyone else was here, they'd probably be like, 'But Exusiai, you also love raising hell too!'
It sure sucks having to do this alone.
What's this? Someone need a helping hand?
I've been wondering, do you, like... have way too much time on your hands?
I'm just like you. We're all waiting for an answer.
What do you mean by that?
Does it make a big difference to you whether or not Texas goes back to Lungmen?
Absolutely.
And as of right now, I want her to go back.
Hmm... Does that mean you're on our side?
Sure does.
Great. Can't party alone.
Sorry, sorry. I shouldn't have said all that depressing stuff.
However, it should be clear to everyone by now that I'm not here to give an inaugural speech.
I came here to speak from my heart.
Carracci, as bold as his vision was, never mentioned anything to me about his outlook regarding the new city.
The reason for that is, when he took up his position, he was already prepared to die.
We've all borne witness to what he did over the past two years.
And just when I thought he might make it long enough to see the birth of the new city, he suddenly died a few days ago.
As for me, that was the last straw.
So, I cozied up to Famiglia Bellone, recommended myself to them, and obtained this position.
In that process, I discovered a shocking conspiracy the Bellone and Saluzzo famiglie have been brewing—
From the very beginning, they plotted to plunge this city into chaos and seize the newly-built nomadic city.
Then, they will seek to leverage it against Signora Sicilia.
Don Alberto, this—
Any news from Bernardo yet?
No, Don Alberto.
What's the meaning of this, Bernardo?
Danbrown.
Danbrown!
...I'm here.
You're near Rubio's location, right?
Yes.
Whack that lunatic.
Yes, Don Alberto.
Alright... I guess I can't keep you out, if you really want to come in.
You're an idiot.
What's the point of all this?
The way you're dressed... Are you an ordinary worker?
I work at a carwash. It's called 'Auto Lavaggio Danbrown.'
Oh, that rings a bell.
Yes.
So, you must be Danbrown then?
That's me.
Do you have any particular concerns about your job?
No.
Siracusa's gotten soggy and stale. I've had nothing to do, so I found some work for myself.
That's a shame. You should've looked a little harder.
You arrogant wise guys think you can lord over us forever.
You think your only enemies are each other, you never take any of us seriously.
But, the times are changing. Nothing stays the same forever.
No matter how nice your words might sound, they're just words.
Can words overthrow the famiglie, or Signora Sicilia?
No, but I've taken this once-in-a-lifetime opportunity and said what I needed to say.
As for who'll win between the Bellones and Signora Sicilia, I don't know, or care.
All I know is—this new era has no room for the likes of you.
If the next chapter of Siracusan history is about to be written, the heroes of that story won't be the famiglie.
We may have no power. We may indeed be weak.
But we know that civilization rose to overcome this savagery.
We pursue a form of equality, an order not rooted in violence.
Violence is in our nature.
Really now? Why, then, are all the famiglie moving in the same direction?
Why are they chasing money and power instead of violence and conflict?
Is killing me today just a cathartic release of this violence?
Or is it because... I got in the way of the people behind you taking power?
See, you already know the answer.
And, if you don't mind, I'd like to be the one to finish myself off.
Do you know what this is?
A gun?
That's right. I purchased it from Blacksteel a few years ago, through my connections.
It's hard to use, and it's not very useful against a pack of wise guys lunging at you.
All it can do is offer a little protection to one as weak as myself.
By the looks of things, I already figured out who I should be pointing it at years ago.
Rubio slowly raises the gun's muzzle to his temple.
Oh right, Danbrown.
I hope that one day, you can just be a regular carwash worker here in Siracusa. One who never has to hold a weapon, ever again.

lines.st: October 19th
How I yearn to unload everything on my chest in a speech.
But, I cannot.
I have to convince them that I decided to take revenge on the famiglie because of Carracci's death.
Plus, there are some words that cannot be spoken yet.
I'm not here to usher in a new era. I'm not arrogant enough to think that.
I don't even know how many hearts my words will be able to reach.
But, I believe that as long as this country still has people like me, Carracci, and Lavinia...
Then there is still hope for Siracusa.
So, I will write what I wanted to say here:
Having said so much about Carracci, the reality is, as you may realize, I'm not here to talk about Carracci.
I'm here to talk about myself. And all of us, my dear citizens.
We live in a cage called the famiglie.
They may not always directly affect our lives, but they are always a part of it.
Every ordinary person in Siracusa, over the course of their lives, will come to gain their own understanding of the famiglie through various ways.
And eventually, they get used to it.
However, I hope you can take a good look at the people around you, and look back at your own life.
There is one thing we all should understand—
We are human beings. Our lives are very much real. We are not livestock in a pen.
And above all, we are powerful.
Our power comes from the very lives we have created.
The lives the mafiosi relish in were also created by our hands.
This is the country we've built, brick by brick.

lines.x: Judge Lavinia, am I dreaming? Why is my father... talking with a hitman like that?
No, father! He's in danger!
